I just wish the level up screen was more soulful but everything else is great. The music is pretty good if a bit forgettable, The rpgs mechanics are amazing in this one and the devs allow you to really go wild with different builds, I love the character variety though I do wish mushroom kingdom characters had more variance with looks and costumes. the game also can be challenging especially towards the end. The only big flaws is the weird framerate dips which feel un-mario and also the pacing. The game would've been perfect if it ended when you defeated Zokkett. A good return to form for this franchise but way longer than it should be. The animation and battle system is great in this game though:

Good game overall, but sadly not fun enough to justify the huge time investment. (We’re talking 50+ hours for the main story + side quests.) Combat often feels boring and repetitive—there’s no nuance or strategy, 99% of the time you just spam your strongest move (which have agonisingly long, unskippable animations by the way. These get longer and longer as you unlock more powerful moves) and wait for it to be over. Sidequests include the usual offenders of fetch quests, walking simulator quests, and, in a couple of cases, loot grinding. Which is a shame because the story is genuinely interesting. The game does have a good soul, it’s just hampered by boring, repetitive combat and generic sidequests that pad the length out far longer than it needed to be.

REVIEW: EXCELLENT GAME ️At first, I thought it would be boring — I even started to regret my purchase. But soon the game completely hooked me! I realized it’s not just about RPG battles; every move makes you think, but in a fun and engaging way. The best part is the story — just when you think the game is about to end… BOOM!  It surprises you!I found it to be a really great game, I totally recommend it. 5 stars ️

Mario & Luigi: Brotherhood Connection recovers the spirit of the series with its humor, dynamic combat, and cooperative mechanics, but it fails to surprise or reinvent itself. Although it is an entertaining adventure, the jump to 3D subtracts some of the visual charm, the music goes unnoticed, and the difficulty is too low to pose a real challenge.Furthermore, performance issues can affect fluidity in key moments. It's not a bad game, but it's not a memorable return either; fans will enjoy it, but it leaves the feeling that it could have been much more.

1. The graphic is a more simplistic cell-shading style which I'm not sure if I like. Still, I can say it does weird me out sometimes and it REALLY bothers me that Mario and Luigi have this weird blue tint on their clothes. 2. Fighting is good but it annoys that you have little ways to avoid  battles with weak enemies and it's still round based. If you don't like it, this game won't do anything to change your mind. Eventually you also will end up to constantly using the strongest brother attacks and skill and equip your characters to optimize their use, just to get quicker through the many battles you can hardly avoid, not matter if the enemy is strong or weak. 3. Each of the major bosses has unique abilities and mechanics and if you haven't mastered the dodge mechanic facing them, you will have a bad time. It is fun how different they are however. 4. On different islands you have different actions and gimmicks to go through to finish it and it's nice and varied so it never overstays it's welcome but some of them can get real infuriating, especially on subsequent visits for completion. One especially enraging is the rhythm mini-game that keeps on getting worse and it gave me a real bad time but it might just be me that has an issue with it but it was bad enough for me to drop the rating one extra point. 5. The “plug” system works nice at first, allows to adapt to situation and is just rightly restricted  but as you craft more of them, it becomes a bit redundant because the way you end up playing and the enemies you face on the regular becomes narrow and the recharge is a bit long and some plugs you hardly ever use, only as a “just to have something until it recharges” case. 6. Overall it's a pretty good new entry of the series and a good return but it has a few issues and doesn't revolve or evolve the series but also doesn't do anything wrong so there is no reason to avoid it, unless you hate the kind of game it is.

Nintendo really likes bringing back old things just for nostalgia. However, I've never played a Mario&Luigi game before this one so I'm not gonna be driven by emotions. First things first, it looks really nice, I like the new artstyle, it's refreshing. The combat system is really fun and I find myself going towards enemies just to play battles. The story is great, I like the plot twist at the end and it's overall a pretty good story for a Mario game. I wanted togive Mario & Luigi: Brothership a 9, but I think that towards the end of the game the pacing starts to slow down and you get bored of esploring and just playing. All in all, I think it's one of the best Mario RPG experiences you can buy on the Switch thanks to its presentation, story and beatiful mechanics

Brothership is a solid entry into the Mario & Luigi series with fun combat, an intriguing (if somewhat contrived) storyline, and relatively satisfying boss battles and secret-hunting elements. However, it lacks the strong, idiosyncratic humor from previous iterations like "Partners In Time" or "Bowser's Inside Story", the difficulty level leaves a lot to be desired, and there is little-to-no incentive to replay the game. 

I did not take any damage until the first boss, and I did not have either of the Bros faint until 12-13 hours into the game, which was easily remedied by one of the dozen-or-so 1-UPs I had accumulated thus far. I didn't once get a "GAME OVER" during my playthrough. Mushrooms were more of a sellable commodity than a useful mid-battle heal, and the defensive gear options seemed inconsequential when I wasn't taking any hits in the first place. I understand this may not have been the typical experience, but, at least for me, the game felt way too easy. It's okay to make the game accessible to fans that are less experienced with RPGs, some difficulty options (sliders, toggles, really ANYTHING) for veterans like myself would have been welcomed with open arms.

The story this time around was acceptable, but little more than that. It also felt somewhat contrived, and it crucially lacked some of the punchy, oddball humor that other games in the series had plenty of. The key side characters felt more like plot devices than actual living, breathing elements that genuinely interacted with the Bros, and the world was little more than a series of locations designed to advance the story. Each new island offered little in the way of unique storytelling elements; one by one, Mario and Luigi would travel there, plug in the giant plug, and leave. Very few of the "levels" left a lasting impact on me, and I felt no desire (outside of the requirement for sidequests) to revisit any of them.

All this to say, the game ISN'T BAD. Likewise, however, it's not much more than DECENT. In some ways, it feels half-baked (particularly the story and character development), and I don't see myself coming back to this one a couple of years down the road to revisit the story or re-explore the islands. Fans had to wait a long time for this game, and it's a bit disappointing that Nintendo couldn't deliver a more fulfilling, engaging experience. Add a difficulty slider or hard mode, and I'd be all over it, but until then I won't be returning to Concordia any time soon. I'd give it a 7.4 if I could, but I suppose a 7 is closer to what I'm feeling here than an 8.

Mario & Luigi: Brothership is a solid addition to the franchise, offering a fun and nostalgic experience with innovations that refresh the gameplay. Despite some minor performance issues and a difficulty level that could be higher for experienced players, the game stands out for its visual presentation, music, and game mechanics.

Mario and Luigi: Brothership Review — 6.7/10

Mario and Luigi: Brothership feels like that one group project where everyone had good ideas, but no one really finished their part. The base gameplay is fine — battling is fun enough to keep you awake, but it’s nothing you’ll be telling your friends about over dinner. For a Mario & Luigi game, it’s surprisingly... mid.

The biggest problem? Repetition. After a while, it feels like you're doing the same thing over and over, like being stuck on an endless treadmill of "pretty good" ideas that never really go anywhere. It's like the game wanted to be a grand adventure, but someone hit "save and exit" halfway through development.

The story and concept are actually decent, and you can tell there was passion behind this. But it’s hard to shake the feeling that Brothership plays more like a demo for a bigger, better game that never showed up.

In the end, it's not terrible. It's not great. It's just okay. Hardcore fans might enjoy it, but casual players might wonder why they didn’t just replay an older Mario & Luigi game instead.

Final Verdict: Like getting a free sample at the store — tastes good, but not enough to buy the full product

Pros:A highly enjoyable, old-school turn-based RPG experience.Epic and innovative boss battles that keep players engaged.Outstanding soundtrack and sound effects that enhance the overall atmosphere.
Cons: Many side quests lack excitement and feel monotonous.Regular combat becomes somewhat repetitive after approximately 30 hours of gameplay.

Mario & Luigi: Brothership was an interesting game to say the least. It had many good qualities but also quite a few bad ones. For context this was my first Mario & Luigi game, so I hadn’t played any prior. Highlights of the game include its story, characters and overarching mechanics (linking islands and Luigi logic). I thought it provided a charming and wholesome story with amazing characters to support it, I especially liked Connie and Technikki. I found exploring and linking new islands to be extremely enjoyable and a fun mechanic. However one major downfall of this game is how long it went on for, it took me 40+ hours to complete no Mario and Luigi title should take that long to finish and at many parts it felt like the game would never end and it was a chore to play. Another small thing that very much annoyed me was the STUPID tree animation that would play after literally anything and everything, it did not need to be there it added absolutely nothing and consistently wasted my time. But overall, Mario & Luigi: Brothership is a solid game with subtle flaws. As long as you push through some of the boring bits you’ll have an enjoyable experience.

hello my dear viewers, happy mario day (well actually happy mario week because mario day has already passed). but anyway, for the mario week special i'm gonna review the return to the greatest RPGS in videogame history: Mario and Luigi Brothership. now unlike most of my reviews where i comment about the flaws and positives, i will talk about IGN's review of this game. there's a conspiracy going on surrounding IGN about this game, i might aswell check the review to figure out the conspiracy. This is a return to a great RPG series, so we can assume that IGN is gonna give this game a.... 5?! only a 5?! The new mario and luigi game just got it's ### handed to it by IGN! well thats fine, because i will keep chasing down IGN with my pitchfork and torch. see IGN is known for their reviews, they rate games depending on how they feel, telling people to avoid games that are bad, but instead most of those reviews are complete lies! like concord, dragon age veilguard and this game. this review damaged the game's reputation, so i'm gonna fix it, by reviewing ign's review about this game. the first flaw that IGN said is that it's the worst mario RPG game on the switch, or infact the worst mario game on the switch because it got the lowest score of all the mario games on the switch. i don't think it's the worst one, what i consider the mario game to be the worst of them all is Mario Strikers Battle League, and i already told you how bad that game is. but IGN just gave that game an 8, but this game they gave it a 5, that doesn't make any sense! second flaw is the sailing in this game, IGN said that he didn't like it because it's not wind waker. just because it's not like in wind waker doesn't mean it's bad.  like who the f### cares? we all like the sea exploration no matter if it's wind waker-ish or not (actually i do, but only at the beginning, because the ship moves so slow that i will be old man by the time i'll get to a new island, but once you get a way to speed the ship up, it's not a problem). 3rd: IGN said that this is "babies' first rpg". what's made for babies? is it the difficulty? the exploration? either way, it's made for everyone hence the rating "rated E for everybody". 4th: handholding dialogue. according to IGN, the dialogues keep holding your hand again and again throughout the game. but really, Why is Logan Plant having so much trouble skipping the cutscenes? He doesn't know he can speed up the dialogue by holding the B button, The reviewer **** at skipping cutscenes more so than any human being regardless of age or experience could possibly just forget the skip button. the irony just baffles me. 5th: the story. IGN apparently just hates the story because the writing is unoriginal. okay, the writing for me is just perfection. trust me, the writing is great and original, just don't listen to him. 6th: the characters. okay this is just ridiculous. IGN didn't like the characters from this game, let alone original characters. remember when alot of people hated Sticker Star? isn't it pretty clear that Original characters is important for RPGs? 7th: generic locations. most of the islands you visit are mostly ice, fire, sand, etc. it doesn't even count as generic, it's the same aesthetics over and over again. how many settings could you think of other than grass, fire or ice? 8th: perfomance issue. look i'm using an OLED model of the switch, and the only performance issue i could find was the loading screen. because how does this affect the whole game anyway? it's not like the Glitch Gremlin f##### up this game's performance more so than in Pokemon Scarlet and Violet, so the game is running smooth. 9th: Luigi works differently. this is the only IGN complaint i agree on, because Luigi works different here, in most mario and luigi games he follows you around and can be a second character you control, but here, it's like there's a second player controlling him. he follows you at an awkward angle, he can jump on his own, and to top it off, you can't control him by swapping with the "-" button. okay this is the last complaint about ign in this review: the pacing. he said that the third act added nothing of substance. but i don't feel disappointed about it. because it's one of the best third acts in videogame history. on par with Marvel's Spider Man's 1 and 2 act 3. alright that's all the flaws i could mention about ign's review of this game, this rating might have hurt the game, but i fixed it with this review, anyways, don't listen to what ign says, just listen to what i say in these metacritic scores, anyway, hope this review fixed the reputation and forget about the ign rating of this game, just take it and throw it away. anyways i consider this game to be the 2nd best RPG game on the switch, well i'm running out of characters so i might aswell end the review right here, so... peace out. 9/10

However, it lacks the strong, idiosyncratic humor from previous iterations like "Partners In Time" or "Bowser's Inside Story", the difficulty level leaves a lot to be desired, and there is little-to-no incentive to replay the game. 

I did not take any damage until the first boss, and I did not have either of the Bros faint until 12-13 hours into the game, which was easily remedied by one of the dozen-or-so 1-UPs I had accumulated thus far. I didn't once get a "GAME OVER" during my playthrough. I understand this may not have been the typical experience, but, at least for me, the game felt way too easy. It's okay to make the game accessible to fans that are less experienced with RPGs, some difficulty options (sliders, toggles, really ANYTHING) for veterans like myself would have been welcomed with open arms.

The story this time around was acceptable, but little more than that. It also felt somewhat contrived, and it crucially lacked some of the punchy, oddball humor that other games in the series had plenty of. The key side characters felt more like plot devices than actual living, breathing elements that genuinely interacted with the Bros, and the world was little more than a series of locations designed to advance the story. Each new island offered little in the way of unique storytelling elements; one by one, Mario and Luigi would travel there, plug in the giant plug, and leave. Very few of the "levels" left a lasting impact on me, and I felt no desire (outside of the requirement for sidequests) to revisit any of them.

All this to say, the game ISN'T BAD. Likewise, however, it's not much more than DECENT. In some ways, it feels half-baked (particularly the story and character development), and I don't see myself coming back to this one a couple of years down the road to revisit the story or re-explore the islands. Fans had to wait a long time for this game, and it's a bit disappointing that Nintendo couldn't deliver a more fulfilling, engaging experience. Add a difficulty slider or hard mode, and I'd be all over it, but until then I won't be returning to Concordia any time soon. Mario & Luigi: Brothership is a solid addition to the franchise, offering a fun and nostalgic experience with innovations that refresh the gameplay. Despite some minor performance issues and a difficulty level that could be higher for experienced players, the game stands out for its visual presentation, music, and game mechanics.

Mario and Luigi: Brothership Review — 6.7/10

Mario and Luigi: Brothership feels like that one group project where everyone had good ideas, but no one really finished their part. The base gameplay is fine — battling is fun enough to keep you awake, but it’s nothing you’ll be telling your friends about over dinner. For a Mario & Luigi game, it’s surprisingly... mid.

The biggest problem? Repetition. After a while, it feels like you're doing the same thing over and over, like being stuck on an endless treadmill of "pretty good" ideas that never really go anywhere. It's like the game wanted to be a grand adventure, but someone hit "save and exit" halfway through development.

The story and concept are actually decent, and you can tell there was passion behind this. But it’s hard to shake the feeling that Brothership plays more like a demo for a bigger, better game that never showed up.

In the end, it's not terrible. It's not great. It's just okay. Hardcore fans might enjoy it, but casual players might wonder why they didn’t just replay an older Mario & Luigi game instead.

Final Verdict: Like getting a free sample at the store — tastes good, but not enough to buy the full product

Pros:A highly enjoyable, old-school turn-based RPG experience.Epic and innovative boss battles that keep players engaged.Outstanding soundtrack and sound effects that enhance the overall atmosphere.
Cons: Many side quests lack excitement and feel monotonous.Regular combat becomes somewhat repetitive after approximately 30 hours of gameplay.

Mario & Luigi: Brothership was an interesting game to say the least. It had many good qualities but also quite a few bad ones. For context this was my first Mario & Luigi game, so I hadn’t played any prior. Highlights of the game include its story, characters and overarching mechanics (linking islands and Luigi logic). I thought it provided a charming and wholesome story with amazing characters to support it, I especially liked Connie and Technikki. I found exploring and linking new islands to be extremely enjoyable and a fun mechanic. However one major downfall of this game is how long it went on for, it took me 40+ hours to complete no Mario and Luigi title should take that long to finish and at many parts it felt like the game would never end and it was a chore to play. Another small thing that very much annoyed me was the STUPID tree animation that would play after literally anything and everything, it did not need to be there it added absolutely nothing and consistently wasted my time. But overall, Mario & Luigi: Brothership is a solid game with subtle flaws. As long as you push through some of the boring bits you’ll have an enjoyable experience.

Good game overall, but sadly not fun enough to justify the huge time investment. (We’re talking 50+ hours for the main story + side quests.) Combat often feels boring and repetitive—there’s no nuance or strategy, 99% of the time you just spam your strongest move (which have agonisingly long, unskippable animations by the way. These get longer and longer as you unlock more powerful moves) and wait for it to be over. Sidequests include the usual offenders of fetch quests, walking simulator quests, and, in a couple of cases, loot grinding. Which is a shame because the story is genuinely interesting. The game does have a good soul, it’s just hampered by boring, repetitive combat and generic sidequests that pad the length out far longer than it needed to be.

I realized it’s not just about RPG battles; every move makes you think, but in a fun and engaging way. The best part is the story — just when you think the game is about to end… BOOM!  It surprises you!I found it to be a really great game, I totally recommend it. 5 stars ️

Mario & Luigi: Brotherhood Connection recovers the spirit of the series with its humor, dynamic combat, and cooperative mechanics, but it fails to surprise or reinvent itself. Although it is an entertaining adventure, the jump to 3D subtracts some of the visual charm, the music goes unnoticed, and the difficulty is too low to pose a real challenge.Furthermore, performance issues can affect fluidity in key moments. It's not a bad game, but it's not a memorable return either; fans will enjoy it, but it leaves the feeling that it could have been much more.

1. The graphic is a more simplistic cell-shading style which I'm not sure if I like. Still, I can say it does weird me out sometimes and it REALLY bothers me that Mario and Luigi have this weird blue tint on their clothes. 2. Fighting is good but it annoys that you have little ways to avoid  battles with weak enemies and it's still round based. If you don't like it, this game won't do anything to change your mind. Eventually you also will end up to constantly using the strongest brother attacks and skill and equip your characters to optimize their use, just to get quicker through the many battles you can hardly avoid, not matter if the enemy is strong or weak. 3. Each of the major bosses has unique abilities and mechanics and if you haven't mastered the dodge mechanic facing them, you will have a bad time. It is fun how different they are however. 4. On different islands you have different actions and gimmicks to go through to finish it and it's nice and varied so it never overstays it's welcome but some of them can get real infuriating, especially on subsequent visits for completion. One especially enraging is the rhythm mini-game that keeps on getting worse and it gave me a real bad time but it might just be me that has an issue with it but it was bad enough for me to drop the rating one extra point. 5. The “plug” system works nice at first, allows to adapt to situation and is just rightly restricted  but as you craft more of them, it becomes a bit redundant because the way you end up playing and the enemies you face on the regular becomes narrow and the recharge is a bit long and some plugs you hardly ever use, only as a “just to have something until it recharges” case. 6. Overall it's a pretty good new entry of the series and a good return but it has a few issues and doesn't revolve or evolve the series but also doesn't do anything wrong so there is no reason to avoid it, unless you hate the kind of game it is.

Nintendo really likes bringing back old things just for nostalgia. However, I've never played a Mario&Luigi game before this one so I'm not gonna be driven by emotions. First things first, it looks really nice, I like the new artstyle, it's refreshing. The combat system is really fun and I find myself going towards enemies just to play battles. The story is great, I like the plot twist at the end and it's overall a pretty good story for a Mario game. I wanted togive Mario & Luigi: Brothership a 9, but I think that towards the end of the game the pacing starts to slow down and you get bored of esploring and just playing. All in all, I think it's one of the best Mario RPG experiences you can buy on the Switch thanks to its presentation, story and beatiful mechanics

Brothership is a solid entry into the Mario & Luigi series with fun combat, an intriguing (if somewhat contrived) storyline, and relatively satisfying boss battles and secret-hunting elements. However, it lacks the strong, idiosyncratic humor from previous iterations like "Partners In Time" or "Bowser's Inside Story", the difficulty level leaves a lot to be desired, and there is little-to-no incentive to replay the game. 

I did not take any damage until the first boss, and I did not have either of the Bros faint until 12-13 hours into the game, which was easily remedied by one of the dozen-or-so 1-UPs I had accumulated thus far. I didn't once get a "GAME OVER" during my playthrough. Mushrooms were more of a sellable commodity than a useful mid-battle heal, and the defensive gear options seemed inconsequential when I wasn't taking any hits in the first place. I understand this may not have been the typical experience, but, at least for me, the game felt way too easy. It's okay to make the game accessible to fans that are less experienced with RPGs, some difficulty options (sliders, toggles, really ANYTHING) for veterans like myself would have been welcomed with open arms.

The story this time around was acceptable, but little more than that. It also felt somewhat contrived, and it crucially lacked some of the punchy, oddball humor that other games in the series had plenty of. The key side characters felt more like plot devices than actual living, breathing elements that genuinely interacted with the Bros, and the world was little more than a series of locations designed to advance the story. Each new island offered little in the way of unique storytelling elements; one by one, Mario and Luigi would travel there, plug in the giant plug, and leave. Very few of the "levels" left a lasting impact on me, and I felt no desire (outside of the requirement for sidequests) to revisit any of them.

All this to say, the game ISN'T BAD. Likewise, however, it's not much more than DECENT. In some ways, it feels half-baked (particularly the story and character development), and I don't see myself coming back to this one a couple of years down the road to revisit the story or re-explore the islands. Fans had to wait a long time for this game, and it's a bit disappointing that Nintendo couldn't deliver a more fulfilling, engaging experience. Add a difficulty slider or hard mode, and I'd be all over it, but until then I won't be returning to Concordia any time soon. I'd give it a 7.4 if I could, but I suppose a 7 is closer to what I'm feeling here than an 8.

Good grief. I get that this is a new team doing this, but this is the most tedious and disjointed Mario and Luigi game to date. The controls have went from this simplified tight control scheme, "Oh we may as well use all the buttons," set ups. The regular attacks are a chore after 4hrs in (every attack doesn't need to be a Bros attack). The staging of game is nonsense. You go to do something only to accidentally walk into a random cutscene and need to listen to another piece of the non-existant plot. On top of this the currents are one of the worst ways to travel around the map. Also they do give you fast sailing, but you need to play 5hrs of the game beforehand. Potential is through the roof but overall its a poorly paced, tedious rpg, that has brilliant ideas but is ultimately over developed spoiling the experience.

Next time. Map Luigi the B button in all instances (particularly battles), lock your cutscenes behind character interactions,  never limit the travel via currents and don't make basic attacks bros attacks.

Update 26/01. I swear I'm now 10hrs in. Dragging. All above issues compounding. But the worst thing is, I have another 30hrs to go if I want to complete it. Most Mario and Luigi RPGs take 26-30hrs. Good grief. So much padding.......

Brothership morphs into a slogging disappointment in the later stages of the game. What starts off as really enthralling gameplay, breaks down into confusion, repetition, frustration and regret during the last few islands. Stumbling with really horrible near-end boss fights like The Great Conductor my advice to anyone playing this game, finish up after you beat Bowser for the second time. Thank me later.

Well Nintendo messed up, don't get the positive reviews, but they need to look harder for flaws, the vattle plugs ruined the game for me, auto excellent? What!? Even in the first half the game is slow and pretty tedious, the joke aren't funny and repeat too often, and some gameplay elements butcher the game.

Battle plugs make the game easy, for the wrong reasons, first of all auto excellent should be removed from the game, its skilless, and does not really encourage the player to do much, and learn a bros move for themselves, making tutorials on bros moves pointless, the spikeball plug is also kinda broken, overall I think that plugs were not the way to go here, and Should've been adjusted, and nerfed a little, but this game wants to be ridiculously easy soooo.. yea.

The game is beautiful and pretty cute but it presents no challenge and requires no attention, which makes it rather dull and boring. I think the game is aimed at children, and the level of puzzles, writing, and humor support that. The story has no real stakes. I'm tired of Nintendo only making games that present no challenge and constantly try to reward the player as if it were one of those stupid mobile games. I started playing video games when I was 7 years old with Super Mario World on the Super Nintendo. Back then, video games taught us perseverance and thinking. It held our attention and gave us a sense of accomplishment and satisfaction. That's not the case anymore and it's really sad.
Mid game, only better than paperjam sorry for m&l fans this game is worse than you spect

This game is a huge steaming pile of garbage. The greedy developers are charging $60 for a mobile game. The graphics are a joke. This whole "art style" stuff is just an excuse for developers to be cheap and lazy. When I pay $60 I expect top-notch AAA quality, no exceptions. I don't want to hear any excuses about the Switch being underpowered, because isn't it supposed to be more powerful than the PS3? Then why do the footprints in the snow and sand disappear so quickly? 3 seconds? In GTA V and TLOU1, the footprints remain on the sand for a much longer period of time, and that was on PS3 hardware. That is embarrassing. The trails look so fake, it literally looks like someone accidentally spilled beige paint on green-colored paper and called that spilled overlay a trail. The iris is missing from Mario and Luigi's eyes. The gameplay is boring and repetitive. These cheapskates used the laziest form of combat, and that's turn-based combat. That way, the lazy developers don't have to worry about working on the enemy AI. Despite all the shortcuts these developers took to create this shovelware mobile game, it still suffers from performance issues. ● There is a big canon that is equipped with a telescope, and its purpose is to access the islands. When using the telescope to search for an island, you can clearly see that the eyepiece view is circular. That implies that the optical lens are round; obviously they're supposed to be round. But right when I chose to launch onto an island, I was horrified when I saw that the optical tube and lens had a polygon shape. When I used the telescope for a second time, I paid close attention to see if my eyes were deceiving me. And as I took a closer look I was able to see that the tube and lens had, what appeared to be, a decagon shape. When I saw that, I immediately grabbed my chair and threw it against the wall and walked off. I'm aware that Nintendo makes nothing but indie quality games, but this is taking it too far. This is completely unacceptable for a 2024 $60 game. I was debating whether to quit this game at that point, but I eventually continued. The rope bridge does not wobble when you walk on it, and it doesn't react to your movements at all. When I smashed giant rocks, the broken pieces magically fall under the ground. This is a $60 game, where is the detail? ● There is no multiplayer. So once you beat the story, there is literally not a reason to play this game again. Charging $60 for a game with no replay value is a cash grab. The warp pipe has 2 pairs of footprints, suggesting co-op was in development, but the lazy devs tapped out to multiplayer. Since Luigi is useless in this game and acts pretty much as a companion, multiplayer could've fixed that problem. There were sections that looked accessible but I was met with invisible walls. The animations are laughable. The physics are trash. The attacks are awful. The Clockout Blow is the absolute worst, because half of the time the hammer is not hitting the enemies, yet they take damage. And what's with the black outlines on the characters? I paid $60, I do not want to see any contour lines at that price, or at least where it's not applicable. When I pay $60, I expect a combat system with depth and detail. All the developers did in this game is put pretty lights during every move, to give players the illusion that the game is fun. The developers also added cute upbeat music to give players the illusion that the game is fun. None of that is fun for me. I don't recommend this game at all.

No surprise, it's another modern Nintendo game that cares more about looking like a silly little kid's show than actually doing anything to be a good game. Nintendo used to at least try and strike a good balance of challenge and interesting aesthetic to appeal to all ages, but that era is long gone. With Brothership Nintendo stands firm in their post golden-age philosophy: design for 5 year olds who don't really care about videogames. This means dropping any level of challenge, dull exploration, constant stopping for drawn out cutscenes, droning dialogue, lack of tone and mechanics that made the previous games good, and a cheesy toddler-ized style you'd find on low-budget kid's TV. This creates a bland, menial, impossible-to-fail walkthrough experience that struggles to hold a child's attention, let alone any RPG fan's.

The dialogue is bland and corperate. 
I wasn't expected bowser to be all:HEY JERKWARDS! What do you two boys think yah doing messin with me huh? Think your lame hammers can break down my manly fortress?? You must be goin' crazy from its deadly fumes! HAHAHAHHA wipe those tears off your faces and come back to fight when you know how to strike a REAL BLOW.
But there is NO enemy dialogue that goes beyond typical and safe.
Regardless of this, the animations is beautiful and the gameplay is...mediocre. I noticed right away how unsatisfying it was to fight when compared to other M&L games. The feeling of impact when striking an enemy just isnt there and bro moves are few and spaced out.

Gorgeous art style and soundtrack aside, Mario & Luigi: Brothership simply leaves a lot to desire in comparison to its predecessors. The gameplay takes way too long to introduce interesting battle mechanics, some controls have been changed for no reason, there are performance issues, enemy and environment designs are bland, and the handholding isn't quite as bad as Origami King or Tearaway Unfolded, but is still quite annoying nonetheless. The story being set in another world makes things even less relevant/enjoyable, as there's no worldbuilding expansion, one of Superstar Saga's and Bowser Inside Story's best features.

I will give a bit of credit for the devs actually creating a new world for the game to take place in.  It's very pretty, but that is about the only nice thing I can say about the game.  Combat is really samey and doesn't evolve much as the game progresses. The movelist is very very repetitve and lacks the creativity seen in earlier titles.  The world is well animated but the writing is, well, bland.  The Mario RPGs live and die on their characters, the writing, and the pacing, but this game story is just so unbearably uninteresting and flat.  Also, they /literally/ copy and pasted the intro from the recent Dreamworks Mario movie into this game to start the game off. The laziness on display is staggering.

M&L series continues to ship out uninspired story beats and slow pacing. Thankfully the battling remains Solid what but everything else false flat. The game holds your hand more than any other entry in the series. It’s almost insulting.

I can understand IGN's ratings, but I still think they rated the game too well. If I had to give a rating: 3/10. This is by far the worst installment in the series, surpassing even Paper Jam Bros. Among other things, I find the new Mario and Luigi voice actor insufferable and nerdy, tarnishing the reputation of Mr. Martinet. I couldn't get a picture with in Wonder because I only played as Daisy.I know I've only written negative things now, but there's really nothing good I could write about it.
A game that is as long as is equally boring. The best part is the few mario rpg aspects of battle. Most missions are strung out far too long for no real reason the game is not really fun.
Boring game and a lot talking make the game more boring the gameplay in not that bad i dont like the style of gameplay

One of the worst games in the series. Outdated gameplay, boring characters, annoying voice sfx, amateurish graphics, and too much text. Cutscenes interrupt every two steps. Slow pacing. No offense, but this is one of Nintendo's worst games. I've tried it several times now, but I just can't get excited about the game. I hope the next game will be better, I actually like this series.

this game was so bad I gave up after the first main lighthouse. this was the most boring nothing game I've ever played. Luigi does way to much and it's shoved into your face whenever luigi has something he can do. the character designs are all bland and boring and all look the same.  leveling up is something that just happens and it doesn't feel like your getting that much stronger. it also never feels like you actually have to but any items at all. the battle system has way to much unnecessary stuff thrown into it. they decided to go for quantity over quality for all the islands and it shows. it feels like nintendo was trying to use the hype and another mario and luigi game to sell this one but it just didn't work. IGN was right.

Shocked at how poor this was, the opening is so slow and the gameplay is outdated by about 2 decades. There is no humor or thought in any of the dialogue, the 2 leads are more annoying than I can ever remember them.
Repetitive, laggy controls due to low fps, sometimes stressful in battles where if you don't kill all the monsters in one shot (sometimes difficult due to input lag) you have to stay there for hours.

What a trash game. Runs at 20-15 fps and demands you to react with extreme precision in every fight to avoid enemy attacks. Story is trash mobile game looking garbage. Avoid at all cost. Trust me. I played it for 25 hours. U dint want to do that.

Good grief. I get that this is a new team doing this, but this is the most tedious and disjointed Mario and Luigi game to date. The controls have went from this simplified tight control scheme, "Oh we may as well use all the buttons," set ups. The regular attacks are a chore after 4hrs in (every attack doesn't need to be a Bros attack). The staging of game is nonsense. You go to do something only to accidentally walk into a random cutscene and need to listen to another piece of the non-existant plot. On top of this the currents are one of the worst ways to travel around the map. Also they do give you fast sailing, but you need to play 5hrs of the game beforehand. Potential is through the roof but overall its a poorly paced, tedious rpg, that has brilliant ideas but is ultimately over developed spoiling the experience.

Next time. Map Luigi the B button in all instances (particularly battles), lock your cutscenes behind character interactions,  never limit the travel via currents and don't make basic attacks bros attacks.

Update 26/01. I swear I'm now 10hrs in. Dragging. All above issues compounding. But the worst thing is, I have another 30hrs to go if I want to complete it. Most Mario and Luigi RPGs take 26-30hrs. Good grief. So much padding.......

mario-kart-world_with_metadata.json.review: Online mode is boring when it forces you into transitional courses, the open world mode is repetitive, especially when you're going for the challenges, it feels more like a chore than an accomplishment and the achievement earnings are underwhelming when they're just stickers. This game is not worth the $80 price.

Mario kart world is a great kart racer and one of the best in the series, it delivers in terms of content however the tracks can be a bit boring, it has great driving mechanics but the open world mode it a bit lack luster
Just worse in every way than mk8deluxe. It's more expensive, has less features, has worse tracks, and a useless open world. Looks pretty though.

It’s just a kart game—there isn’t much room to innovate here, just stick to the formula that already works. It has plenty of characters and fun tracks, although I think the game launched with too few track options and not enough variety. I don’t believe the open-world mode is necessary or useful; they could have invested in making more maps instead of that mode.That said, it’s still a great game. I have a lot of fun playing it, and without a doubt it’s the game I’ve played the most on the Switch 2.

I only have one point of criticism and that is the Grand Prix, the sprints are not so fun

Otherwise top

For this to be touted as the quintessential opener for the Switch 2 is beyond me. This may be the most halfbaked MarioKart to date. The more environmental use approach doesnt translate well for a Mario Kart game. Everything is so wide and open that its awkward, even on 150cc the game feels more like a cruise rather than a "race" due to this. While the graphics see a boost, the various animations leave much to be desired.

Boosting and wall riding mechanics are annoying to pull off. The drifting is far too exaggerated. Items dont really add anything new, just more coin based ones for the most part. Huge segments of tracks are transistional adding next to nothing to the gameplay, especially in multiplayer where you will spend the majority of your time in them. I could be tricked into thinking it was either a well designed loading screen, or lazy in game design. 

Being in first place also doesnt seem to matter as with so many players on the track you will inevitably to screwed by a blue or gold shell. This game is the worst offender out of all of them at item spamming and there is no way to customize a race. The stickers you unlock are lame and you can only place one on your cart. Also while there is a giant roster tons of them are just alt versions of the same character. Free play mode has next to no reason to bother with it. Battlemode is a huge missed oppurtunity and is so safe to the formula that it introduces only the same two modes ever seen.

Since I got this as a pack in title with my Switch 2 im only midly let down... If I paid $80 for this I would be livid. I never thought I would dislike a Mario Kart game. For this price this should not be one of the most boring racing games ive ever owned. Charging a 1 point (3 to 2) audacity tax on the review score since Nintendo believes this is worth $80. Kirby Air Riders (and I cant believe im saying this) has everything I actually wanted in a Nintendo racing game and is more affordable.
Not the best Mario Kart, but the most expensive one. Nintendo price policy is getting ridiculus
Its fun but not as good as mario kart 8 imo. Not only that, I also think the levels are kind of boring and the game just feels slower

As a Mario Kart-Fan since SNES i am very disappointed. Boring open world concept, boring tracks - most of them dont even have turns(!!!). Speed is too slow, even slower when you have to drive through water. Worst of all the carts feel like trucks. Tight turns with low speed are just impossible, what makes the open world tasks even more frustrating. And there is no visual improvement to MK8.

I absolutely love Mario Kart World and I don’t really understand all the hate on it. It is the slickest and smoothest gas in the series with the most detailed and complex tracks and mechanics to create an easy to pick up but hard to master experience for diehards and newcomers. 9.5/10

I never played Mario Kart before, so it's probably my mistake for choosing this game. I wanted to get some fun racing game with chaos, like Rock'n'Roll Racing. But this is not that kind ****. It's beautiful, but idiotic. You can't see characters stats, you have over 5 hundred of collectibles without any way to find and track them properly, but these are only minor issues. I wanted to get a racing game where you can win because of driving skills, reaction, buildup and being smart. Instead of it I got the racing game that forces you to learn and practice parkour shortcuts for hours if you want to win. Apart from this, it's already confirmed that AI in this game is built the way, that if you don't use any game mechanics(drift, tricks, rail riding), it'll be much easier to win. It's absolutely lazy and stupid.

The game is flawed but amazing. Imo I think overhated. It brings a whole new open world alongside racing. Knockout tour is my personal favorite with its battle royal racing hybrid and the tense feeling of fighting for qualification is really challenging and fun. I will say intermissions do bring a whole new style that a lot of people (including me sometimes) don't like with its line like tracks. And 80$ is absurd. It's only worth it with the bundle with 50$ but still kinda greedy. Overall it's amazing but can use some tweaks.
Very great details but lack of battle modes and some characters aren't there.

Mario Kart World still has room to improve — more interactions with the environment, more curves so the tracks don’t feel too monotonous in elimination mode, and maybe some long-term performance stats. I’d love to know how many times I’ve won, how often I finished in the top 3, and which combinations I use the most… even so, it’s still the best Mario Kart ever made.

absolute garbage. a pathetic insult to the mario kart games. cant even bother to run without constant frame drops, intermissions are a joke, 200cc removed, awful custom rules, trash UI, poor visual clarity, uninspired and repetitive OST. go play MKWII or MK8

This is the most disappointing game I have played in over 30 years. The core gameplay is (almost there, drifting is not great), graphics are amazing, the game itself just ****, lacking in everything. No story mode?? Close to 0 race options online, the world was used wrong, straight roads… no GP online (without intermissions), pointless world, it just feels half arsed, incomplete, lack of tracks, 24 players is too chaotic, 12 was spot on in MK8. It’s very frustrating, I wanted this game to be so much more than it is.

Mario Kart World feels very different from Mario Kart 8 and is easily the most ambitious entry in the series so far. With 24 racers on the track, the game fully leans into chaos. Races are intense and unpredictable, but there are moments where the chaos goes too far and turns into frustration **** of the game’s biggest strengths is its visual overhaul. The more cartoonish art style stands out, and the animations are excellent. Characters feel way more expressive, alive, and visually fresh than MK8 also gives the game a lot of personality. The soundtrack is another major highlight. The music is fantastic, with great compositions and remixes that are among the best in the **** idea of interconnected tracks is ambitious and interesting. Making them feel natural and fluid is not easy, but for the most part, the game succeeds. Transitions between areas are smooth, and this structure works especially well in modes like Survival and, to some extent, Grand Prix. The new jumping mechanics are optional, which is a good decision, since you can still play in a classic Mario Kart style without being forced to change how you race.That said, this is also where many of the game’s problems begin. While interconnected circuits sound great in theory, they do not work well in Grand Prix mode. Instead of racing three full laps on a single track, races are turned into short tour-style routes. Most of the time, these feel like transition races between circuits rather than full tracks, making them feel too short and less important. In VS mode, the game also forces interconnected circuits, removing player choice completely.Because of this, the entire game feels overly dependent on the connected-track structure. This approach really only works in Survival mode, while other modes suffer from it. The lack of classic modes is disappointing, and there is very little customization. You cannot choose which circuits to connect or tailor the experience to your liking, which hurts **** open world is another major disappointment. Considering the game is literally called Mario Kart World, expectations were high, but the open world feels empty and lifeless. Instead of being a core feature, it feels like a side mode with no real purpose. There is little reason to explore, since the rewards are mostly stickers that you rarely even see. Unlocking costumes is repetitive and annoying, and overall the open world feels more like a beta or demo than a fully developed feature.Despite having one of the best soundtracks in the game, there is no way to freely listen to the music or properly adjust the audio. You can’t lower sound effects to better enjoy the music, which is especially frustrating in such a chaotic game. While the chaos can be fun, it often becomes tiring or even borderline unplayable.Mario Kart World clearly has a lot of work behind it, but in its current state it feels unfinished. It does not justify a $80 price tag. Personally, I would pay around $40 for it and hope the game improves through free updates rather than paid DLC. 7/10
It’s just boring. I keep going back to play Mario Kart 8 Deluxe. There is less skill involved in Mario Kart World, drifting is less important.

Great game. Very different if you compare it to MK8.
Track knowledge is key in this game. Once you learn the tracks and how to use it to you advantage,the game opens up.
I can't believe how much I dislike this game. I feel cheated by Nintendo. I wish I didn't buy this bundled with the console because I want to sell it.

I played Mario Kart 8 Deluxe on the original Switch for about 40 hours. It was a good game to play with friends, but it never truly hooked me personally. When I bought the Switch 2, I debated keeping the old cartridge as a backup. However, after trying Mario Kart World, I made the decision to sell MK8 immediately. I simply cannot go back.I don’t understand the hate this game receives. Yes, I acknowledge the downsides: fewer tracks and characters at launch, no kart customization, and the "Open World" concept feels slightly underutilized (I wish there were 1v1 duels or custom route creation in the open hub). But despite these flaws, the game is a massive step **** new physics and graphics are game-changers. The driving feels so much better that the previous entry feels obsolete by comparison. The trick system and shortcuts are fantastic. But the real star here is the Marathon (Elimination) Mode. It is infinitely more dynamic and fun than the classic "3 laps" formula. Continuous driving keeps the flow going and holds my attention in a way the old games never **** the first time, I find myself launching Mario Kart to play solo, not just when guests come over. It has also become a great way for my wife and me to spend time together—we play online and locally, and it’s always a blast.This is a logical evolution of the series. I hope future DLCs will expand the open-world activities and roster, but as it stands, this is the game I keep coming back to on my Switch 2.
It’s a fun time with friends and family though I wish the open worldhad more to do and Being able to play a single track online but it’s not that bad ****. Also worth $80? No.

A great Mario Kart title but with a few hiccups. The controls, graphics, and frame rate are all great but the problems lie in certain decisions made for game modes. This title lacks the traditional 3 lap tracks for online play. It's a mix between intermission and normal races which doesn't give players a concrete choice on game mode type. Knockout tour on the other hand is a fantastic addition to the game and adds a lot more depth to the online races. The open world does lack real collectables and should have had a bit more to explore for. Currently the open world aspect isn't that engaging other than P switch challenges which thankfully you can track on the minimap now. Overall a great game with some jarring choices but always a fun time with friends, family or online as usual with Nintendo titles.

It’s pretty good
But I do think Crossworlds is better
But definitely one of the best Mario karts

It's almost poetic, not to say tragic, that after decades of burning virtual rubber since that 1992 Super Nintendo, the reward for our loyalty is a shiny, empty package. I've lost count of the laps I've done in this franchise; if there were a stopwatch that recorded every drift since the 16-bit era, a million laps would be a milestone I'd earned. That's why receiving the Nintendo Switch 2 along with the brand-new Mario Kart World wasn't a cause for celebration, but rather the beginning of a clinical autopsy. After two weeks of postponing the inevitable, the disappointment isn't just real, it's palpable. Now I understand the deathly silence of the honest press and the freefall of the hype that once sustained this brand. Nintendo has sold us a luxury mausoleum, a wax museum where everything looks immaculate, but nothing **** first slap of reality comes with the absence of the number. That "9" we were all expecting has been replaced by a "World" that reeks of cheap marketing and creative laziness. There's no number because there's no progress; it's a tacit admission that innovation has died at the hands of comfort. The game is an endless procession of ghosts, a reinterpretation of tracks we've already ridden to death. For those of us with Mario Kart DNA in our blood, this title isn't a new experience, it's a constant déjà vu, a facelift for tracks we already know by heart before the developers of this game were even born. It's the triumph of the graphic filter over the architecture of the design. It looks better, yes, with the visual maturity afforded by the new hardware, but what good is a silk suit on a lifeless body?The sacrilege continues with the character roster and the absolute simplification of the mechanics. In its eagerness to please everyone, Nintendo has ended up respecting no one. We can drive anything from a cow to a dolphin, in a parade of absurdities that feels more like an identity crisis than a range of options. The depth that made Mario Kart 8 a game with more variety—from choosing the chassis and wheels to the hang glider, which defined your destiny on the track—has been eradicated. Now it's all skins. Everything is cosmetic. They've turned a fun arcade racing game into a Mario Odyssey-style costume **** don't even get me started on the track structure. The supposed "connection" between circuits that gives the game its name is a failed experiment that feels forced and artificial. Moving from track one to track two and so on doesn't flow like an organic journey; it's a poorly executed, blobbed-out nostalgia, accompanied by classic musical arrangements that, while adequate, lack the necessary emotional core to make you feel like you're experiencing something epic. I simply didn't connect with this **** there's one thing to applaud, it's that Nintendo has decided that playing well is no longer a requirement. The inclusion of automatic items behind the vehicle is the final proof that this game wasn't designed for us, those of us who grew up holding down the L button until our fingers went numb to protect our position with a banana or a shell. Now, the game does it for you. It's "easy mode" made standard. It's the democratization of success for those who can't (or won't) learn the basic rules of survival on the track. Add to that the fact that the penalty for being in first place has been reduced, with fewer blue shells hitting the leader, and you're left with a tame and tension-free **** conclusion, Mario Kart World is a monument to greed and the law of least effort. It's a disguised remake, a reinterpretation for a generation that didn't experience the glory of the previous games. The only track that seems to attempt something new is the Donkey Kong circuit in the Mushroom Cup, an oasis of creativity in an otherwise repetitive desert. For those of us who grew up in the nineties, this title is a disappointment. It's functional, it's pretty, and it's massive, but it lacks the authenticity that only comes from a desire to experiment and remain true to the brand's identity. RATING 7/10
I don't play Mario Kart online, so I haven't experienced the same trauma and insult as other players, I guess. It's a great game, and you should play with your friends or family split screen.

Abit overpriced in my opinion, the mario kart maps are some of the best ive ever seen with quite alot og variety and shortcuts which i love. Custimization wise feels like a downgrade form earlier games. Free roam is pretty but not much to do. Knockout is quite fun andf fits 24 players, but 24 players for normal cup is way too much. The road between maps is very boring to drive through and very straight forward.
Wonderful rainbowroad, best game to play with my wife. Not price matter but happy game experience

Overall, my impression is that this is a very boring game. All the tracks are uninteresting and not memorable, and 150cc feels too slow, especially considering that the tracks have a lot of completely straight sections. All the tracks kind of lack any interesting style; for example, in Mario Kart 8 all the tracks were different and felt distinct, each had its own theme and was memorable, but in Mario Kart World in single-player, in each new race you first drive out of the previous track and then end up on a new one, and because of that the courses feel even more blurred in terms of level style. I really hope the developers fix this with new tracks and also add 200cc.

This was my initial review. But the more I played, and the more I started using all the game’s mechanics, such as rails and walls, the more I began to like the game.

Mario Kart World Is Very Overwhelming,
Its Unique mechanics Are Almost Not Used In Normal Gameplay, It is A Massive Down Grade Compared To The Game That Came Before It (Mario Kart 8 Deluxe), You Can't Choose Your Wheels Or Gliders, Most Characters Don't Have any Skins, There Are Less Roads And  Less Cups , And The New Items Are not So Fun To The Gameplay, It Seems More Like A Generic Kart Game Other Than Mario Kart.

Game is seriously isn't worth it for a launch title. Even though the gameplay is ok, it is considered to be a massive disappointment/letdown compared to Mario Kart 8/8 Deluxe. The open world is bland, intermission tracks, and barely any new game modes besides Knockout Tour, no extra battle mode content, no 200cc, and the unworthy $80 price for such little content. Such a shame Nintendo had to ruin this game in the stupidest way possible.
The only 2 positives i can point out to the game is the stunning soundtrack and great graphics, but other than that, don't buy it if you don't want a game that has barely any content at all.

Positives: So much variety with the charachter roster, great music, the best mechanics for a MK game ever with wall riding and rail riding. The learning curve to get really good at this game and master it is steep but very rewarding when you can pull it off. Fun tracks and great art style to the game.

Negatives: The online experience ... can't pick 3 lap tracks and you are forced to play intermission tracks online which are so boring. The online rooms hardly fill up probably because people have become fed up with being force fed intermission tracks... i had to rely on discord servers to be able to play 3 lap tracks consistently..Open world Free roam mode gets boring very quickly. 24 players online is too chaotic.. should cap it at 12 players like before. No DLC and limited amount of 3 lap tracks.. MK8 deluxe has nearly 100 !!
Best Rainbow Road in History. Amazing tracks, songs and environment. So much fun with my friends.

So basically that's like an experiment that I HOPE is going to evolve through time. It is a slightly improved version of 8 Deluxe but some mechanics, the open world and lack of content are questionable at this point.
Gameplay Mechanics - 7/10: Controls are smooth and you can adapt quickly to new ways of driving on the rails and walls (the other question is - who needs them?); new intermission courses is an interesting new touch for this type of racing game but I'd prefer it to stay in the Knockout Tour only (especially they are annoying in multiplayer races, bring back the laps style only!); all the items are fun to use, balance is OK. The open world.... ehhh it's simply empty dude, I hope developers are working on something more to that. It's supposed to be the game's MAIN feature but i feels more like a bonus one and the least amount of people are gonna use Free Roam...
Graphics & Visuals - 10/10: All is great, scaling is perfect. 
Sound & Music - 7/10: Honestly, I haven't got into any tune here yet... Maybe it's because there are yet another bunch of remixes of the same stuff and because of the new intermission courses the music now has to be more flexible and thus kinda blurry and unmemorable.
Replayability - 10/10: No question about that here, it's always gonna be super replayable in any state and especially with multiplayer. 
Innovation - 7/10: It has some new ideas (new regime, new mechanics, new type of courses, new items, open world) but the quality and necessity of them are questionable.
Technical Performance - 10/10. As any Nintendo game - perfectly polished. Multiplayer works excellently.
Art Direction/Design - 10/10: Quickly got into the cartoonish and juicy aesthetics, very appealing and fun.
Monetization/Value - 8/10: Hard to say if it's worth the price. Definitely for the fans of the series it is. But I really hope that in the future we'll see more FREE patches and even DLCs that gonna expand the game and polish it to the state of the previous installment, so it's finally worth the price.
All in all it's a bit arrogant of developers to say that this game is so huge that you have to pay this much, while I feel that this game is a bit underdeveloped actually.

Total - 9/10.

Mario Kart World successfully evolves the long-running franchise while staying true to its core identity. The expanded tracks, refined mechanics, and lively presentation create an experience that feels both fresh and comfortably familiar. Each race feels dynamic, rewarding skill while still embracing the chaotic fun the series is known **** game shines in its variety, with creative course design and strong multiplayer appeal that keeps sessions engaging over time. While there are minor areas that could be improved, they do little to detract from the overall experience.Mario Kart World stands as one of the strongest entries in the series, offering an exciting and polished racing experience that is easy to recommend to both longtime fans and newcomers alike. with the only downside being...battle mode. Battle Mode is the weakest part of Mario Kart World because it feels underdeveloped compared to the rest of the game. The arenas lack the creativity and chaos of the race tracks, and matches can feel repetitive pretty quickly. It’s not bad, but it doesn’t reach the same level of polish or excitement as the main racing experience.

The most boring and dull Mario Kart and kart racer regardless to date. The online mode forces to play linear matches through transitional courses, the open world feels barebones and more like a chore than an accomplishment. I really don't get how people think this is superior than Sonic Racing: CrossWorlds. You're better off playing that great game than this $80 price slugfest.

Mario Kart World is decent, but lacks innovative track design and any semblance of balance. Rubber-banding is always present in Mario Kart games, but it can get bad. Mario Kart 64 may be the worst, though. I have a few burning questions: Why do the cc’s matter at all for difficulty? Why can’t we just select the cc’s and difficulty as separate things? What happened to mirror mode or 200 cc mode? Why don’t they just finally do a Nintendo Kart and have Link, Zelda, Captain Falcon, Fox, Kirby, Samus, Diddy, etc. instead of ridiculous characters like baby characters and NPC-type characters? Why did they limit the amount of options for gameplay? Why is the A button used for accelerate? So I can get carpal tunnel in my thumb? Mario Kart World has such potential, but at the end of the day, Mario Kart 8 is better in every way. Mario Kart World feels… souless.
Finally got a switch 2 and got Mario kart bundle and me and kids can’t stop playing it , loved 8 but this has so much more . Will be playing for a while and absolute blast to play

Online mode is boring when it forces you into transitional courses, the open world mode is repetitive, especially when you're going for the challenges, it feels more like a chore than an accomplishment and the achievement earnings are underwhelming when they're just stickers. This game is not worth the $80 price.

Mario kart world is a great kart racer and one of the best in the series, it delivers in terms of content however the tracks can be a bit boring, it has great driving mechanics but the open world mode it a bit lack luster
Just worse in every way than mk8deluxe. It's more expensive, has less features, has worse tracks, and a useless open world. Looks pretty though.

It’s just a kart game—there isn’t much room to innovate here, just stick to the formula that already works. It has plenty of characters and fun tracks, although I think the game launched with too few track options and not enough variety. I don’t believe the open-world mode is necessary or useful; they could have invested in making more maps instead of that mode.That said, it’s still a great game. I have a lot of fun playing it, and without a doubt it’s the game I’ve played the most on the Switch 2.

I only have one point of criticism and that is the Grand Prix, the sprints are not so fun

Otherwise top

For this to be touted as the quintessential opener for the Switch 2 is beyond me. This may be the most halfbaked MarioKart to date. The more environmental use approach doesnt translate well for a Mario Kart game. Everything is so wide and open that its awkward, even on 150cc the game feels more like a cruise rather than a "race" due to this. While the graphics see a boost, the various animations leave much to be desired.

Boosting and wall riding mechanics are annoying to pull off. The drifting is far too exaggerated. Items dont really add anything new, just more coin based ones for the most part. Huge segments of tracks are transistional adding next to nothing to the gameplay, especially in multiplayer where you will spend the majority of your time in them. I could be tricked into thinking it was either a well designed loading screen, or lazy in game design. 

Being in first place also doesnt seem to matter as with so many players on the track you will inevitably to screwed by a blue or gold shell. This game is the worst offender out of all of them at item spamming and there is no way to customize a race. The stickers you unlock are lame and you can only place one on your cart. Also while there is a giant roster tons of them are just alt versions of the same character. Free play mode has next to no reason to bother with it. Battlemode is a huge missed oppurtunity and is so safe to the formula that it introduces only the same two modes ever seen.

Since I got this as a pack in title with my Switch 2 im only midly let down... If I paid $80 for this I would be livid. I never thought I would dislike a Mario Kart game. For this price this should not be one of the most boring racing games ive ever owned. Charging a 1 point (3 to 2) audacity tax on the review score since Nintendo believes this is worth $80. Kirby Air Riders (and I cant believe im saying this) has everything I actually wanted in a Nintendo racing game and is more affordable.
Not the best Mario Kart, but the most expensive one. Nintendo price policy is getting ridiculus
Its fun but not as good as mario kart 8 imo. Not only that, I also think the levels are kind of boring and the game just feels slower

As a Mario Kart-Fan since SNES i am very disappointed. Boring open world concept, boring tracks - most of them dont even have turns(!!!). Speed is too slow, even slower when you have to drive through water. Worst of all the carts feel like trucks. Tight turns with low speed are just impossible, what makes the open world tasks even more frustrating. And there is no visual improvement to MK8.

I absolutely love Mario Kart World and I don’t really understand all the hate on it. It is the slickest and smoothest gas in the series with the most detailed and complex tracks and mechanics to create an easy to pick up but hard to master experience for diehards and newcomers. 9.5/10

I never played Mario Kart before, so it's probably my mistake for choosing this game. I wanted to get some fun racing game with chaos, like Rock'n'Roll Racing. But this is not that kind ****. It's beautiful, but idiotic. You can't see characters stats, you have over 5 hundred of collectibles without any way to find and track them properly, but these are only minor issues. I wanted to get a racing game where you can win because of driving skills, reaction, buildup and being smart. Instead of it I got the racing game that forces you to learn and practice parkour shortcuts for hours if you want to win. Apart from this, it's already confirmed that AI in this game is built the way, that if you don't use any game mechanics(drift, tricks, rail riding), it'll be much easier to win. It's absolutely lazy and stupid.

The game is flawed but amazing. Imo I think overhated. It brings a whole new open world alongside racing. Knockout tour is my personal favorite with its battle royal racing hybrid and the tense feeling of fighting for qualification is really challenging and fun. I will say intermissions do bring a whole new style that a lot of people (including me sometimes) don't like with its line like tracks. And 80$ is absurd. It's only worth it with the bundle with 50$ but still kinda greedy. Overall it's amazing but can use some tweaks.
Very great details but lack of battle modes and some characters aren't there.

Mario Kart World still has room to improve — more interactions with the environment, more curves so the tracks don’t feel too monotonous in elimination mode, and maybe some long-term performance stats. I’d love to know how many times I’ve won, how often I finished in the top 3, and which combinations I use the most… even so, it’s still the best Mario Kart ever made.

absolute garbage. a pathetic insult to the mario kart games. cant even bother to run without constant frame drops, intermissions are a joke, 200cc removed, awful custom rules, trash UI, poor visual clarity, uninspired and repetitive OST. go play MKWII or MK8

This is the most disappointing game I have played in over 30 years. The core gameplay is (almost there, drifting is not great), graphics are amazing, the game itself just ****, lacking in everything. No story mode?? Close to 0 race options online, the world was used wrong, straight roads… no GP online (without intermissions), pointless world, it just feels half arsed, incomplete, lack of tracks, 24 players is too chaotic, 12 was spot on in MK8. It’s very frustrating, I wanted this game to be so much more than it is.

Mario Kart World feels very different from Mario Kart 8 and is easily the most ambitious entry in the series so far. With 24 racers on the track, the game fully leans into chaos. Races are intense and unpredictable, but there are moments where the chaos goes too far and turns into frustration **** of the game’s biggest strengths is its visual overhaul. The more cartoonish art style stands out, and the animations are excellent. Characters feel way more expressive, alive, and visually fresh than MK8 also gives the game a lot of personality. The soundtrack is another major highlight. The music is fantastic, with great compositions and remixes that are among the best in the **** idea of interconnected tracks is ambitious and interesting. Making them feel natural and fluid is not easy, but for the most part, the game succeeds. Transitions between areas are smooth, and this structure works especially well in modes like Survival and, to some extent, Grand Prix. The new jumping mechanics are optional, which is a good decision, since you can still play in a classic Mario Kart style without being forced to change how you race.That said, this is also where many of the game’s problems begin. While interconnected circuits sound great in theory, they do not work well in Grand Prix mode. Instead of racing three full laps on a single track, races are turned into short tour-style routes. Most of the time, these feel like transition races between circuits rather than full tracks, making them feel too short and less important. In VS mode, the game also forces interconnected circuits, removing player choice completely.Because of this, the entire game feels overly dependent on the connected-track structure. This approach really only works in Survival mode, while other modes suffer from it. The lack of classic modes is disappointing, and there is very little customization. You cannot choose which circuits to connect or tailor the experience to your liking, which hurts **** open world is another major disappointment. Considering the game is literally called Mario Kart World, expectations were high, but the open world feels empty and lifeless. Instead of being a core feature, it feels like a side mode with no real purpose. There is little reason to explore, since the rewards are mostly stickers that you rarely even see. Unlocking costumes is repetitive and annoying, and overall the open world feels more like a beta or demo than a fully developed feature.Despite having one of the best soundtracks in the game, there is no way to freely listen to the music or properly adjust the audio. You can’t lower sound effects to better enjoy the music, which is especially frustrating in such a chaotic game. While the chaos can be fun, it often becomes tiring or even borderline unplayable.Mario Kart World clearly has a lot of work behind it, but in its current state it feels unfinished. It does not justify a $80 price tag. Personally, I would pay around $40 for it and hope the game improves through free updates rather than paid DLC. 7/10
It’s just boring. I keep going back to play Mario Kart 8 Deluxe. There is less skill involved in Mario Kart World, drifting is less important.

Great game. Very different if you compare it to MK8.
Track knowledge is key in this game. Once you learn the tracks and how to use it to you advantage,the game opens up.
I can't believe how much I dislike this game. I feel cheated by Nintendo. I wish I didn't buy this bundled with the console because I want to sell it.

I played Mario Kart 8 Deluxe on the original Switch for about 40 hours. It was a good game to play with friends, but it never truly hooked me personally. When I bought the Switch 2, I debated keeping the old cartridge as a backup. However, after trying Mario Kart World, I made the decision to sell MK8 immediately. I simply cannot go back.I don’t understand the hate this game receives. Yes, I acknowledge the downsides: fewer tracks and characters at launch, no kart customization, and the "Open World" concept feels slightly underutilized (I wish there were 1v1 duels or custom route creation in the open hub). But despite these flaws, the game is a massive step **** new physics and graphics are game-changers. The driving feels so much better that the previous entry feels obsolete by comparison. The trick system and shortcuts are fantastic. But the real star here is the Marathon (Elimination) Mode. It is infinitely more dynamic and fun than the classic "3 laps" formula. Continuous driving keeps the flow going and holds my attention in a way the old games never **** the first time, I find myself launching Mario Kart to play solo, not just when guests come over. It has also become a great way for my wife and me to spend time together—we play online and locally, and it’s always a blast.This is a logical evolution of the series. I hope future DLCs will expand the open-world activities and roster, but as it stands, this is the game I keep coming back to on my Switch 2.
It’s a fun time with friends and family though I wish the open worldhad more to do and Being able to play a single track online but it’s not that bad ****. Also worth $80? No.

A great Mario Kart title but with a few hiccups. The controls, graphics, and frame rate are all great but the problems lie in certain decisions made for game modes. This title lacks the traditional 3 lap tracks for online play. It's a mix between intermission and normal races which doesn't give players a concrete choice on game mode type. Knockout tour on the other hand is a fantastic addition to the game and adds a lot more depth to the online races. The open world does lack real collectables and should have had a bit more to explore for. Currently the open world aspect isn't that engaging other than P switch challenges which thankfully you can track on the minimap now. Overall a great game with some jarring choices but always a fun time with friends, family or online as usual with Nintendo titles.

It’s pretty good
But I do think Crossworlds is better
But definitely one of the best Mario karts
