All in all it's a bit arrogant of developers to say that this game is so huge that you have to pay this much, while I feel that this game is a bit underdeveloped actually.

Total - 9/10.

Mario Kart World successfully evolves the long-running franchise while staying true to its core identity. The expanded tracks, refined mechanics, and lively presentation create an experience that feels both fresh and comfortably familiar. Each race feels dynamic, rewarding skill while still embracing the chaotic fun the series is known **** game shines in its variety, with creative course design and strong multiplayer appeal that keeps sessions engaging over time. While there are minor areas that could be improved, they do little to detract from the overall experience.Mario Kart World stands as one of the strongest entries in the series, offering an exciting and polished racing experience that is easy to recommend to both longtime fans and newcomers alike. with the only downside being...battle mode. Battle Mode is the weakest part of Mario Kart World because it feels underdeveloped compared to the rest of the game. The arenas lack the creativity and chaos of the race tracks, and matches can feel repetitive pretty quickly. It’s not bad, but it doesn’t reach the same level of polish or excitement as the main racing experience.

Why don’t they just finally do a Nintendo Kart and have Link, Zelda, Captain Falcon, Fox, Kirby, Samus, Diddy, etc. instead of ridiculous characters like baby characters and NPC-type characters? Mario Kart World feels very different from Mario Kart 8 and is easily the most ambitious entry in the series so far. With 24 racers on the track, the game fully leans into chaos. Races are intense and unpredictable, but there are moments where the chaos goes too far and turns into frustration **** of the game’s biggest strengths is its visual overhaul. The more cartoonish art style stands out, and the animations are excellent. Characters feel way more expressive, alive, and visually fresh than MK8 also gives the game a lot of personality. The soundtrack is another major highlight. The music is fantastic, with great compositions and remixes that are among the best in the **** idea of interconnected tracks is ambitious and interesting. Making them feel natural and fluid is not easy, but for the most part, the game succeeds. Transitions between areas are smooth, and this structure works especially well in modes like Survival and, to some extent, Grand Prix. The new jumping mechanics are optional, which is a good decision, since you can still play in a classic Mario Kart style without being forced to change how you race.That said, this is also where many of the game’s problems begin. While interconnected circuits sound great in theory, they do not work well in Grand Prix mode. Instead of racing three full laps on a single track, races are turned into short tour-style routes. Most of the time, these feel like transition races between circuits rather than full tracks, making them feel too short and less important. In VS mode, the game also forces interconnected circuits, removing player choice completely.Because of this, the entire game feels overly dependent on the connected-track structure. This approach really only works in Survival mode, while other modes suffer from it. The lack of classic modes is disappointing, and there is very little customization. You cannot choose which circuits to connect or tailor the experience to your liking, which hurts **** open world is another major disappointment. Considering the game is literally called Mario Kart World, expectations were high, but the open world feels empty and lifeless. Instead of being a core feature, it feels like a side mode with no real purpose. There is little reason to explore, since the rewards are mostly stickers that you rarely even see. Unlocking costumes is repetitive and annoying, and overall the open world feels more like a beta or demo than a fully developed feature.Despite having one of the best soundtracks in the game, there is no way to freely listen to the music or properly adjust the audio. You can’t lower sound effects to better enjoy the music, which is especially frustrating in such a chaotic game. While the chaos can be fun, it often becomes tiring or even borderline unplayable.Mario Kart World clearly has a lot of work behind it, but in its current state it feels unfinished. It does not justify a $80 price tag. Personally, I would pay around $40 for it and hope the game improves through free updates rather than paid DLC. 7/10
It’s just boring. I keep going back to play Mario Kart 8 Deluxe. There is less skill involved in Mario Kart World, drifting is less important.

I played Mario Kart 8 Deluxe on the original Switch for about 40 hours. It was a good game to play with friends, but it never truly hooked me personally. When I bought the Switch 2, I debated keeping the old cartridge as a backup. However, after trying Mario Kart World, I made the decision to sell MK8 immediately. I simply cannot go back.I don’t understand the hate this game receives. Yes, I acknowledge the downsides: fewer tracks and characters at launch, no kart customization, and the "Open World" concept feels slightly underutilized (I wish there were 1v1 duels or custom route creation in the open hub). But despite these flaws, the game is a massive step **** new physics and graphics are game-changers. The driving feels so much better that the previous entry feels obsolete by comparison. The trick system and shortcuts are fantastic. But the real star here is the Marathon (Elimination) Mode. It is infinitely more dynamic and fun than the classic "3 laps" formula. Continuous driving keeps the flow going and holds my attention in a way the old games never **** the first time, I find myself launching Mario Kart to play solo, not just when guests come over. It has also become a great way for my wife and me to spend time together—we play online and locally, and it’s always a blast.This is a logical evolution of the series. I hope future DLCs will expand the open-world activities and roster, but as it stands, this is the game I keep coming back to on my Switch 2.
It’s a fun time with friends and family though I wish the open worldhad more to do and Being able to play a single track online but it’s not that bad ****. Also worth $80? No.

A great Mario Kart title but with a few hiccups. The controls, graphics, and frame rate are all great but the problems lie in certain decisions made for game modes. This title lacks the traditional 3 lap tracks for online play. It's a mix between intermission and normal races which doesn't give players a concrete choice on game mode type. Knockout tour on the other hand is a fantastic addition to the game and adds a lot more depth to the online races. The open world does lack real collectables and should have had a bit more to explore for. Currently the open world aspect isn't that engaging other than P switch challenges which thankfully you can track on the minimap now. Overall a great game with some jarring choices but always a fun time with friends, family or online as usual with Nintendo titles.

It's almost poetic, not to say tragic, that after decades of burning virtual rubber since that 1992 Super Nintendo, the reward for our loyalty is a shiny, empty package. I've lost count of the laps I've done in this franchise; if there were a stopwatch that recorded every drift since the 16-bit era, a million laps would be a milestone I'd earned. That's why receiving the Nintendo Switch 2 along with the brand-new Mario Kart World wasn't a cause for celebration, but rather the beginning of a clinical autopsy. After two weeks of postponing the inevitable, the disappointment isn't just real, it's palpable. Now I understand the deathly silence of the honest press and the freefall of the hype that once sustained this brand. Nintendo has sold us a luxury mausoleum, a wax museum where everything looks immaculate, but nothing **** first slap of reality comes with the absence of the number. That "9" we were all expecting has been replaced by a "World" that reeks of cheap marketing and creative laziness. There's no number because there's no progress; it's a tacit admission that innovation has died at the hands of comfort. The game is an endless procession of ghosts, a reinterpretation of tracks we've already ridden to death. For those of us with Mario Kart DNA in our blood, this title isn't a new experience, it's a constant déjà vu, a facelift for tracks we already know by heart before the developers of this game were even born. It's the triumph of the graphic filter over the architecture of the design. It looks better, yes, with the visual maturity afforded by the new hardware, but what good is a silk suit on a lifeless body?The sacrilege continues with the character roster and the absolute simplification of the mechanics. In its eagerness to please everyone, Nintendo has ended up respecting no one. We can drive anything from a cow to a dolphin, in a parade of absurdities that feels more like an identity crisis than a range of options. The depth that made Mario Kart 8 a game with more variety—from choosing the chassis and wheels to the hang glider, which defined your destiny on the track—has been eradicated. Now it's all skins. Everything is cosmetic. They've turned a fun arcade racing game into a Mario Odyssey-style costume **** don't even get me started on the track structure. The supposed "connection" between circuits that gives the game its name is a failed experiment that feels forced and artificial. Moving from track one to track two and so on doesn't flow like an organic journey; it's a poorly executed, blobbed-out nostalgia, accompanied by classic musical arrangements that, while adequate, lack the necessary emotional core to make you feel like you're experiencing something epic. I simply didn't connect with this **** there's one thing to applaud, it's that Nintendo has decided that playing well is no longer a requirement. The inclusion of automatic items behind the vehicle is the final proof that this game wasn't designed for us, those of us who grew up holding down the L button until our fingers went numb to protect our position with a banana or a shell. Now, the game does it for you. It's "easy mode" made standard. It's the democratization of success for those who can't (or won't) learn the basic rules of survival on the track. Add to that the fact that the penalty for being in first place has been reduced, with fewer blue shells hitting the leader, and you're left with a tame and tension-free **** conclusion, Mario Kart World is a monument to greed and the law of least effort. It's a disguised remake, a reinterpretation for a generation that didn't experience the glory of the previous games. The only track that seems to attempt something new is the Donkey Kong circuit in the Mushroom Cup, an oasis of creativity in an otherwise repetitive desert. For those of us who grew up in the nineties, this title is a disappointment. It's functional, it's pretty, and it's massive, but it lacks the authenticity that only comes from a desire to experiment and remain true to the brand's identity. RATING 7/10

Open World Design: The open world feels barren, poorly integrated, and lacks meaningful content, with long, boring stretches of road (intermissions) connecting tracks, Grand Prix Changes: Instead of classic three-lap races, many races are one-lap after driving between courses, making races feel shorter and less traditional, according to IGN and Reddit users.Lack of Content: Key features from 8 Deluxe, like 200cc, underwater driving, and deep kart customization, are missing, disappointing fans.Repetitive Unlocks: Collecting items rewards mostly lazy stickers, with no significant rewards for 100% completion, making exploration feel unrewarding. The $80 price tag felt too high for the amount of content offered at launch, notes Reddit users.Battle Mode: It features far less content than 8 Deluxe, with only two modes compared to five.

For a franchise built on competitive chaos and razor-sharp design, Mario Kart World feels like a disappointing detour. 
Despite new tracks and a bold visual refresh on the Nintendo Switch 2, the game carries an unmistakable sense of downgrade compared to Mario Kart 8 Deluxe. 
The physics feel softer, the tracks more guided, and the AI less dynamic—making each race oddly predictable.Visually, Mario Kart World leans heavily into exaggerated cartoon aesthetics. While colorful and polished, the over-the-top style dilutes the edge and intensity that once made the series such a thrilling party racer. 
The result is a game that feels more aimed at very young audiences than long-time fans craving skill-based **** sense of freedom and tight control that defined Mario Kart 8 is largely missing here. Courses often steer players along rigid paths, minimizing room for risk, shortcuts, or improvisation. The classic spirit of rivalry has been traded for a softer, safer experience—technically smooth, but emotionally flat.

Mario Kart World still delivers moments of fun and nostalgia, but it lacks bite. What was once a clever balance of chaos and control now feels overly curated and cautious. It’s not a crash, just a coasting lap that never quite finds its speed.
The tracks are fun, it adds a few new gimmicks in gameplay, but it still as little replay value. The online modes are still too simple, it needs modern features to customize modes and gameplay.

This game is $80 which is a cash grab. The game is good at the start but it gets boring and the Open World becomes tiring to the point with 24 players on both grand prix and Knockout Tour. I love the new flow of the game, jumping is a really great addition and it really shows on 3-Lap tracks where players find all kinds of shortcuts with it.
Unfortunately, these new mechanics aren't really given the respect they deserve.

The open world could have been great, but it doesn't have a way to track certain collectibles. This makes it difficult to know if you have more to collect, with the only reward for doing so being stickers for the profile picture.

The new Survival Mode consists of really long tracks, where the jump mechanic can't be used a lot and your only choice is to go forward on straight roads, hoping that the other players' items don't make you lose the spot you need to continue with the **** worst part about the game is the multiplayer: there aren't a lot of multiplayer options, and this is a great downside compared to its predecessor.

Firstly, for some reason, tournament mode doesn't exist anymore.
Also, in the online mode, you can't choose if you want to play 3-Lap tracks or the connected open world tracks; this choice doesn't make any sense. It feels like the developers actively try to make people play the boring and long connected tracks (as shown by the updates they made), when they created really nice 3-Lap tracks with shortcuts and good pacing.

This game had the potential to be really good, but after 11 years from Mario Kart 8, a lot of the good things that game had aren't here anymore, and it does not make any sense.

A PRETTY BUT PRICEY STEP SIDEWAYS: Mario Kart World is a beautiful technical showcase for the Nintendo Switch 2, but it’s a tough sell compared to its predecessors and competitors. While the 24-player lobbies are stable, the gameplay feels hollow. Removing the deep kart customization from Mario Kart 8 Deluxe and forcing dull "intermission tracks" into online rotations makes the experience feel like a downgrade. PROS: Stunning 1440p visuals; massive character roster; rock-solid online stability. CONS: $80 price tag; no kart building; boring intermission segments; weak Battle Mode. THE COMPETITION :At $80, MK World is significantly more expensive than Sonic Racing Crossworlds ($70) or Kirby Air Riders ($70). Both competitors offer better value: Kirby includes a full Story Mode, GameShare support, and a Splatoon 3-tier online system! Additionally, Kirby Air Riders allows two local players to race online together on the same screen. FINAL VERDICT: Ultimately, MK World feels like an expensive tech demo. If you want more content and better social features, stick with Kirby Air Riders, Sonic Racing Crossworlds, or the classic MK8 Deluxe.

I’ve always loved this series, and before release I was extremely excited. But I never imagined that only three months after launch, things would turn out this disastrous. Honestly, it’s just heartbreaking. Compared to the energy and excitement of 8 Deluxe, this game already feels lifeless, and online play has been completely broken by the updates.Another major problem is that this game seems to have no real future. The intentions of Nintendo and the players are completely misaligned. Nintendo clearly wants us to enjoy running through the open-world tracks, but the reality is that almost everyone votes for the traditional 3-lap courses, ignoring the unique “world” elements as much as possible. Because of this, even if Nintendo were to release a DLC course pass similar to 8 Deluxe, only the 3-lap courses would ever get chosen, making most of the development budget essentially **** a result, there’s no path where both Nintendo and the players can be happy, and I believe the chances of this game ever regaining momentum are very low. At the very least, I hope Nintendo doesn’t disappoint the fans who truly love this series and even bought a Switch 2 and this game just for it...

I think there are some genuine improvements to the formula with this game but those improvements are entirely overshadowed by the problems and lack of content that this game has. Nintendo's post release response addressed virtually none of the concerns the playerbase had. I bought a switch 2 to play this specifically, and yet, it sits at about 10 hours played. My switch 2 gathers dust now and I definitely have buyer's remorse. In nintendo's own words, the price of their games has been adjusted based on the value and extent of their content. And still, I got bored exploring the free roam after only a few hours. Unlocking characters is not fun at best and a huge, frustrating time waste at worst. 
The driving mechanics are definitely improved and feel responsive.  The intermission tracks that were one of the large features of this game are not fun, and I avoid playing them at all costs. I would not recommend this game even now that it has been out for quite a while. Stay away, I went back to Mario Kart 8, and that's sad.
I’d really loved the game if they went back to the Mario Kart 8 formula. The open world is quite disappointing too and the fact Nintendo isn’t planning DLC right now is shocking to me
I don’t like it at all empty free roam the skill doesn’t matter in this game. Straight drive simulator

This game is a decent Competitive Mario Kart, but it is a bad game filled to the brim with game design flaws. And since MK Studio wanted to make a game for casuals they failed even harder in their mission.
The Open world could have been a great deal for casuals, but due to lack of any conten, it's not.
Casual Couch Multiplayer is better in MK8DX because of multiple reasons:
1. Because of 24 players you almost never see each other in races in MK World. Rivaly is almost impossible. With 12 players in MK8DX this was very different.
2. In MK8DX I, as a better player than most, always had huge leads on my friends. But when they checked my gameplay, they just saw that I took better lines, maybe took some shortcuts, but thats it. In MK World the tech is so insane, that if they would check my gameplay, then they would see that we are just not playing the same game. And that hurts the experience quite a bit when playing together with Casuals.||

So we have established that MK World is a badgame and a bad Casual MK. But even as a competitive MK it is not worth much due to many reasons. Obviously it has the new tech, but thats it. Everything else is worse than in MK8DX. And that is simply not what I expected.
I expected the devs to make online better, make time trial better, make battle mode better, make versus mode better, but everything got worse.

Pettiness is also a huge factor. The lead of this game seems to be an ****.
Yes I want this sentence to be the end of the review.

A good game, but in retrospect, a terrible launch title for the Wii U. It did a poor job demonstrating the console's capabilities, both in terms of HD graphics and utilizing the GamePad in a compelling way. Not to mention the fact that it released the same year as NSMB2, cannibalizing sales between both games. This was never going to be a system seller, (which a first-party launch title should be) considering there were three cheaper, practically identical alternatives available on other consoles.

If this game has anything going for it, it's the challenge mode. I sunk 15+ hours into finishing that alone, and I enjoyed it considerably more than the main campaign. However, there's really not much else notable about it in comparison to the other games in the series, which appears to be a running theme. The NSMB series is so overdone at this point (and was 11 years ago) and this isn't the big refresh 2D Mario needed and the Wii U deserved, which we seem to be finally getting with Super Mario Bros. Wonder.
For me the best game of the New 2d series, great music and level design as always. Very pretty graphics too.

EVEN MORE of the same stuff. We reached a point where its getting really boring now. It is still really fun to play but its so repetitive. Bonus points for multiplayer options and challenge mode. Underwhelming launch title for the Wii U.

I'll give this game a 97%. Nintendo did an excellent job making this game compared to New Super Mario Bros. 2 not being that good of a Mario game in my opinion. This game is more similar to New Super Mario Bros Wii and brought back the Penguin Suit from that game. There are so many unique worlds to go to. Trying to collect star coins throughout the game is a blast! The Acorn suit is a great power-up to use and you can use it to help you collect star coins in certain places as well!

Petty decent game just as most of the Mario bros are, However, I do wish that Nintendo would change up the world themes for once because it does get boring seeing the same thing again and again. What is cool though, is how every level and world has its own unique name for the first time in the series and I do like how the whole 9 world map is all interconnected. The level design is still good and enjoyable. I like the different kind of abstract art style of some of the levels in Soda jungle (world 5) as it looks real cool and kind of like your inside an old painting. Nintendo should definitely use that idea again. The multi player mode is classic as it could be a bonding with friends experience or it could cause fights between them. The propeller suit (though it is severely underused in this game as you can only access it in world 9), ice flower and penguin suits return as power ups in this game along side the new Squirrel suit that makes you able to glide for a short time and they are all fun to use. The music is also decent as well. This game is definitely a huge step up from its mediocre predecessor New super Mario bros 2 on the 3ds. The introduction to baby Yoshi's is also a really cool idea and they are all unique and fun to use. The addition of New super Luigi U is also great as it adds difficulty to this relatively easy game and the challenge mode is fun as well.
i love this game on the wii u i havent 100%ed it and its also my first i want new super lugei u but this will do as one of my favorite wii u games

New Super Mario Bros U came out in the same year as New Super Mario Bros. 2. As a Wii U launch game, it got more copies bought that it would have if it was not a launch game. Right now, you’re probably saying,”Say that again?” It sold better then it should’ve. Does it hold up today?

The story is a little bit different. Instead, PEACH’s castle was taken over. Well, story never defines a platforming game (Much less a Mario game). The gameplay is the same as it always was, and they added a minimal amount of new gimmicks, which really brought the game down. I’m starting to feel like this is just New Super Mario Bros. Wii. There is one new power up and an “infinite” version of that same power up. The Nabbit chases were probably the only original part.

The overall presentation is good, just not memorable. The locations are the same as in Wii and 2. There’s nothing that really stood out to me. The soundtrack is bah-bah-boring, and most of the songs are old, and the new ones are bah remixes of the original Super Mario Bros. soundtrack.

There is quite a bit of content. 3 stars in every level, and if you get a world, you get an extra level. There are some other challenge modes which are a lot of fun, but they are limited and they’re nothing you can’t make in Mario Maker. There are also battle modes against your friends, which I never played because I will never play a 2d platformer with friends.

This game is so similar to Wii, it should be called New Super Mario Bros: Wiihash. If you don’t own any other 2d Mario game or Mario Maker, then maybe you should get this. Not on the Switch though…

A Good 2D Mario.Unpopular opinion but this Is the best NSMB **** world Is connected like SMW the New Power up Is great and the level design Is **** bosses are generic and the music Is catchy but Not the best thing in the **** Challenges are fun

New Super Mario Bros. U is a gem in the Wii U Library and is a must for all Mario fans that own a Wii U. Though the game isn't that different from it's previous titles like New Super Mario Bros Wii. however the game is still filled with some new features like challenge mode and the Game Pad's features that include helping players out by adding platforms and the interactive Boost mode (which sadly I haven't tried out yet due to that I wanted to play the game by myself). It may be a bit disappointing that Nintendo played it safe once again but this is still a solid Mario Title despite that.

This was the last 2D Mario platformer of my marathon. How did it end? Not bad. There was a “new” flying squirrel power up. But really, it was nothing more than a tanooki rip off without the tail whip. I liked how I could view the entire hub world right off the bat, cause it gave me a sense of what to expect, and allowed me to better manage the power ups I had in storage. Speaking of that. The green toad houses put me in a small mini game where two cannons shot 1-ups and bowser faces. Every 1-up I got was a +1 life, and every Bowser I got was a -1 life. The problem was the hitboxes on these cannon balls. Here, you can clearly see the 1-up intercept Toad’s body, yet it wasn’t captured. And another one here. This happened multiple times. I know this was just a bonus mini game, but it none the less still infuriated me cause I got a couple game overs because I was short a life or two, which could have been avoided if the mini game wasn’t broken. Plus, this game reused the bull **** saving system as the other New Super Mario Brother games, meaning I could only save after boss fights. So game overs were extra punishing.

Aside from that, the game basically reiterated most of New Super Mario Bros Wii. Though I did like the obstacle design in this one more. Namely, the long retracting platforms that quite a few levels had. The haunted mansions were never my favorite, and it was no exception here. Like this one in world 6. I was stumped for so long. Had the timer run out multiple times, only to learn the solution was to walk into a wall that actually wasn’t a wall. Sure, this has always been a trademark for the mansions, but I’m sick of it. Forcing the player to randomly walk into walls to progress isn’t fun, at least not for me. A little hint of where the false wall is would have been great.

I don’t know what they can change, but the side scrolling Mario series needs a change. The kind of change we saw from Super Mario 64 to Super Mario Sunshine...then to Galaxy. The “New” Super Mario Bros series isn’t NEW anymore. The title is a blatant lie. It has turned into the Nintendo version of the Call of Duty series. I want something new. Now, the Super Mario Maker series is great, which I’m not getting into in this video, cause that’s more of a player created content peer to peer game. Nintendo, please put an end to the New Super Mario Bros series. I want a truly NEW side scrolling Mario platforming experience.

This game has a lot to love and a lot to hate, I decided to 100% complete the main story so keep that in mind. I think that some pros are that it has loads of content and even some dedicated multiplayer modes which make the multiplayer aspect feel less janky than in previous games, I particularly enjoyed coin battle with family and friends, on top of that the challenge mode is a fun distraction from the campaign. On the topic of the campaign, it's fun to play and complete for the most part with a few annoying elements like losing star coins when you die without a checkpoint. However on the other hand there is an incredible amount of reused assets, animations, and textures from other games. This entry is undoubtedly the best in the New Super Mario Bros. series but it doesn't offer too much more in terms of the core gameplay. Overall, New Super Mario Bros. U looks and plays beautifully but is far too derivative of other games to get anything above a seven without the side content and even with coin battle and challenge mode an 8 is still quite generous. If I could I would give this game a 7.7 out of 10.

How a game as generic, lackluster, and generally unremarkable and boring as this one can obtain a metascore of 84 and a user score of 8.0 is totally beyond me. If you instructed an AI to make a 2D Mario game, this is probably what it would come up with. There are absolutely no surprises to be had here, no new ideas. In fact, the gap in imagination and creativity between the 3D and modern 2D Mario games is close to unfathomable.

The graphics are sub-par even for the Wii U, the music is the most generic drivel imaginable (the Wii U eShop Theme is 10 times better than any song in this game lol), and the level design is incredibly unimaginative. Add to that the typical 2D Mario problems of incredibly slippery movement and inconsequential collectibles and I found very little incentive to keep playing and even finish this game.

This game is a flawless 2D platformer. The length difficulty and content are perfect. People only hate this game because it's not original, and I completely agree with that statement. The game was released at a time when a Mario game came every year, and it doesn't help that it was released on the Wii U. It s an amazing experience, but for many it just came out at the wrong time and place. If you want to play the game than I encourage you to do so, but not for $60, find a discounted Wii U version rather than the Switch port.

New Super Mario Bros. U is a gem in the Wii U Library and is a must for all Mario fans that own a Wii U. Though the game isn't that different from it's previous titles like New Super Mario Bros Wii. however the game is still filled with some new features like challenge mode and the Game Pad's features that include helping players out by adding platforms and the interactive Boost mode (which sadly I haven't tried out yet due to that I wanted to play the game by myself). It may be a bit disappointing that Nintendo played it safe once again but this is still a solid Mario Title despite that.

This was the last 2D Mario platformer of my marathon. How did it end? Not bad. There was a “new” flying squirrel power up. But really, it was nothing more than a tanooki rip off without the tail whip. I liked how I could view the entire hub world right off the bat, cause it gave me a sense of what to expect, and allowed me to better manage the power ups I had in storage. Speaking of that. The green toad houses put me in a small mini game where two cannons shot 1-ups and bowser faces. Every 1-up I got was a +1 life, and every Bowser I got was a -1 life. The problem was the hitboxes on these cannon balls. Here, you can clearly see the 1-up intercept Toad’s body, yet it wasn’t captured. And another one here. This happened multiple times. I know this was just a bonus mini game, but it none the less still infuriated me cause I got a couple game overs because I was short a life or two, which could have been avoided if the mini game wasn’t broken. Plus, this game reused the bull **** saving system as the other New Super Mario Brother games, meaning I could only save after boss fights. So game overs were extra punishing.

Aside from that, the game basically reiterated most of New Super Mario Bros Wii. Though I did like the obstacle design in this one more. Namely, the long retracting platforms that quite a few levels had. The haunted mansions were never my favorite, and it was no exception here. Like this one in world 6. I was stumped for so long. Had the timer run out multiple times, only to learn the solution was to walk into a wall that actually wasn’t a wall. Sure, this has always been a trademark for the mansions, but I’m sick of it. Forcing the player to randomly walk into walls to progress isn’t fun, at least not for me. A little hint of where the false wall is would have been great.

I don’t know what they can change, but the side scrolling Mario series needs a change. The kind of change we saw from Super Mario 64 to Super Mario Sunshine...then to Galaxy. The “New” Super Mario Bros series isn’t NEW anymore. The title is a blatant lie. It has turned into the Nintendo version of the Call of Duty series. I want something new. Now, the Super Mario Maker series is great, which I’m not getting into in this video, cause that’s more of a player created content peer to peer game. Nintendo, please put an end to the New Super Mario Bros series. I want a truly NEW side scrolling Mario platforming experience.

This game has a lot to love and a lot to hate, I decided to 100% complete the main story so keep that in mind. I think that some pros are that it has loads of content and even some dedicated multiplayer modes which make the multiplayer aspect feel less janky than in previous games, I particularly enjoyed coin battle with family and friends, on top of that the challenge mode is a fun distraction from the campaign. On the topic of the campaign, it's fun to play and complete for the most part with a few annoying elements like losing star coins when you die without a checkpoint. However on the other hand there is an incredible amount of reused assets, animations, and textures from other games. This entry is undoubtedly the best in the New Super Mario Bros. series but it doesn't offer too much more in terms of the core gameplay. Overall, New Super Mario Bros. U looks and plays beautifully but is far too derivative of other games to get anything above a seven without the side content and even with coin battle and challenge mode an 8 is still quite generous. If I could I would give this game a 7.7 out of 10.

How a game as generic, lackluster, and generally unremarkable and boring as this one can obtain a metascore of 84 and a user score of 8.0 is totally beyond me. If you instructed an AI to make a 2D Mario game, this is probably what it would come up with. There are absolutely no surprises to be had here, no new ideas. In fact, the gap in imagination and creativity between the 3D and modern 2D Mario games is close to unfathomable.

The graphics are sub-par even for the Wii U, the music is the most generic drivel imaginable (the Wii U eShop Theme is 10 times better than any song in this game lol), and the level design is incredibly unimaginative. Add to that the typical 2D Mario problems of incredibly slippery movement and inconsequential collectibles and I found very little incentive to keep playing and even finish this game.

This game is a flawless 2D platformer. The length difficulty and content are perfect. People only hate this game because it's not original, and I completely agree with that statement. The game was released at a time when a Mario game came every year, and it doesn't help that it was released on the Wii U. It s an amazing experience, but for many it just came out at the wrong time and place. If you want to play the game than I encourage you to do so, but not for $60, find a discounted Wii U version rather than the Switch port.

Although many love to hate on this game and call it a clone of the first three, as my first foray into the world of 2D platformers, I couldn't have asked for a better game to start with. It is highly derivative of its prequels? Absolutely, but that doesn't detract from the fact that it is a very well-crafted and wholesome experience for anyone who decides to pick it up.

A good game, but in retrospect, a terrible launch title for the Wii U. It did a poor job demonstrating the console's capabilities, both in terms of HD graphics and utilizing the GamePad in a compelling way. Not to mention the fact that it released the same year as NSMB2, cannibalizing sales between both games. This was never going to be a system seller, (which a first-party launch title should be) considering there were three cheaper, practically identical alternatives available on other consoles.

If this game has anything going for it, it's the challenge mode. I sunk 15+ hours into finishing that alone, and I enjoyed it considerably more than the main campaign. However, there's really not much else notable about it in comparison to the other games in the series, which appears to be a running theme. The NSMB series is so overdone at this point (and was 11 years ago) and this isn't the big refresh 2D Mario needed and the Wii U deserved, which we seem to be finally getting with Super Mario Bros. Wonder.
For me the best game of the New 2d series, great music and level design as always. Very pretty graphics too.
I just had a Great time playing it and searching for everything the Wii U Gamepad was used well, I think.

EVEN MORE of the same stuff. We reached a point where its getting really boring now. It is still really fun to play but its so repetitive. Bonus points for multiplayer options and challenge mode. Underwhelming launch title for the Wii U.

I'll give this game a 97%. Nintendo did an excellent job making this game compared to New Super Mario Bros. 2 not being that good of a Mario game in my opinion. This game is more similar to New Super Mario Bros Wii and brought back the Penguin Suit from that game. There are so many unique worlds to go to. Trying to collect star coins throughout the game is a blast! The Acorn suit is a great power-up to use and you can use it to help you collect star coins in certain places as well!

Petty decent game just as most of the Mario bros are, However, I do wish that Nintendo would change up the world themes for once because it does get boring seeing the same thing again and again. What is cool though, is how every level and world has its own unique name for the first time in the series and I do like how the whole 9 world map is all interconnected. The level design is still good and enjoyable. I like the different kind of abstract art style of some of the levels in Soda jungle (world 5) as it looks real cool and kind of like your inside an old painting. Nintendo should definitely use that idea again. The multi player mode is classic as it could be a bonding with friends experience or it could cause fights between them. The propeller suit (though it is severely underused in this game as you can only access it in world 9), ice flower and penguin suits return as power ups in this game along side the new Squirrel suit that makes you able to glide for a short time and they are all fun to use. The music is also decent as well. This game is definitely a huge step up from its mediocre predecessor New super Mario bros 2 on the 3ds. The introduction to baby Yoshi's is also a really cool idea and they are all unique and fun to use. The addition of New super Luigi U is also great as it adds difficulty to this relatively easy game and the challenge mode is fun as well.
i love this game on the wii u i havent 100%ed it and its also my first i want new super lugei u but this will do as one of my favorite wii u games
Luigi's a **** in this game and that makes me have jaundice. Btw have you ever played gex

New Super Mario Bros U came out in the same year as New Super Mario Bros. 2. As a Wii U launch game, it got more copies bought that it would have if it was not a launch game. Right now, you’re probably saying,”Say that again?” It sold better then it should’ve. Does it hold up today?

The story is a little bit different. Instead, PEACH’s castle was taken over. Well, story never defines a platforming game (Much less a Mario game). The gameplay is the same as it always was, and they added a minimal amount of new gimmicks, which really brought the game down. I’m starting to feel like this is just New Super Mario Bros. Wii. There is one new power up and an “infinite” version of that same power up. The Nabbit chases were probably the only original part.

The overall presentation is good, just not memorable. The locations are the same as in Wii and 2. There’s nothing that really stood out to me. The soundtrack is bah-bah-boring, and most of the songs are old, and the new ones are bah remixes of the original Super Mario Bros. soundtrack.

There is quite a bit of content. 3 stars in every level, and if you get a world, you get an extra level. There are some other challenge modes which are a lot of fun, but they are limited and they’re nothing you can’t make in Mario Maker. There are also battle modes against your friends, which I never played because I will never play a 2d platformer with friends.

This game is so similar to Wii, it should be called New Super Mario Bros: Wiihash. If you don’t own any other 2d Mario game or Mario Maker, then maybe you should get this. Not on the Switch though…

Fun and creative game. Every level utilized mechanics in different ways to make each level seem distinct and new. I find myself replaying this game often with new challenges every time. Like I’ll try speedruns, no power ups, no running, etc. while I do like making up my own challenges, it’s still fun to just play the game how it was intended. I also admire the fact that the game acknowledges you when you actually 100% the game. They gift you little certificates and I just think that’s sweet.

This is a horrible game. It is just the older ones with different stages. At least with the wii version the final boss was unique in how to defeat him. But in this game he is just a little bit bigger. The stages aren't good, The multiplayer focus isn't great.

New Super Mario Bros. U is probably the best NSMB game. It is very fun, creative, and super. The character selection is good and the levels all feel different and creative. The castle and tower fights are definitely fun, and the Koopalings are more challenging. The artwork is beautiful like 3D World.
It has 4 of the best worlds in any Mario game:
Sparkling Waters, the water world with my favorite ghost house level, great underwater levels, and neat overworld levels.
Frosted Glacier, the snow world with great icy levels, and amazing graphics.
Peach's Castle, no more Bowser's lava world. It's finally the castle that's the final obstacle. This is great and the levels are a joyride. Plus, the story goes from Peach being kidnapped to the castle being invaded and taken over.
and the OG Soda Jungle, 4 different creative sections with every level having great designs, especially Painted Swampland and Deepsea Ruins.
The other worlds, Acorn Plains (no longer the Mushroom Kingdom), Layer Cake Desert, Rock Candy Mines, and Meringue Clouds are also great too.
The Super Acorn and P Acorn are some of the best power ups and the new running up walls feature for the Mini Mushroom is neat as well.

I love NSMBU. It's my 2nd favorite main Mario game after Odyssey.
only better than new mario on wii .. where are all the good ideas and improvement from old games ? i want a nice painted game like supermario world  or mario land 2
This game is basically, just New Super Mario Bros Wii, but on the Wii U, it's just too repetitive

I am very disappointed in Nintendo for making this game.  It plays almost exactly the same as the New SMB for Wii--same sound effects, same music, same graphics (except the upgrade to HD which is not that dramatic given the graphical style of mario games.  Miyamoto said he wanted to release a mario game with each new console, which I agree with, but please do so by making a completely new game, not by just increasing the difficultu and adding a few things.  I don't need my mario games to become ever more complex.  I just need them to be FUN.  And New SMB Wii U is simply not fun if you've already played New SMB Wii.

This game is fun, but I'm giving this a low score for two reasons:

1) This game is basically the same as the original Wii game, just with different levels and a few slightly new features.

2) The whole reason that Luigi exists is because Nintendo needed a second character for 2-player NES games. They also created Waluigi out of thin-air solely because Wario needed a doubles partner in Mario Tennis. So why on earth are we stuck playing with TWO Toads? Why hasn't Nintendo used this opportunity to introduce two new characters to join Mario and Luigi as the "stars" of the game?

Instead of giving us two brand new characters that could be spun off in a ton of other games (not to mention could help increase the character stable for sporting games; who else is sick of all these "baby" characters in Mario Kart? amiright?) they completely blow it and give us not one, but TWO, Toad characters. LAME

Oh well, if you've never played the original Wii game, then this would probably be a fun pickup. If you have played the original Wii game, there really is no reason to get this. It probably should have just been cheap DLC, since, again, it's basically the exact same game, just different levels.

I just recently bought a Wii U with New Super Mario Bros U.  This has been the most poorly constructed Mario game that I can remember.  I enjoyed the New Super Mario Bros for the Wii.  This new Wii U version is the EXACT same thing.  The graphics look identical and the game play feels identical.  If anything, this game is less creative than the former version.  New Super Mario Bros U is a fast cash grabbing solution to a struggling console.

I remember when new mario games by Nintendo EAD were always genre defying AAA epics. It literally feels like i've beaten this game before and it literally feels like Nintendo is lazier than ever.

Too lazy to go with a 4th character instead using a color palette swapped toad, too lazy for actual minibosses reminicent of the yoshis island series, to lazy to put any real heart into their new mario games instead pimping out a cheap SUB-PAR completely acceptable standard product unlike in the olden days before wii fit, wii sports, wii play, and wii party when nintendo actually thought they had to put effort and content into their games for them to sell.

It's wonderful to note that this is the same game with some extra gimmicks. Nothing is developed in this game, nothing feels new or refreshed, nothing introduced as a new concept. it has good feel and great graphics, but if the gameplay is redundant, the music is redundant, the game feels old, what's the damn point of even buying it. I don't recommend if you don't want more of the same from cash-cow mario team.

Too lazy to go with a 4th character instead using a color palette swapped toad, too lazy for actual minibosses reminicent of the yoshis island series, to lazy to put any real heart into their new mario games instead pimping out a cheap SUB-PAR completely acceptable standard product unlike in the olden days before wii fit, wii sports, wii play, and wii party when nintendo actually thought they had to put effort and content into their games for them to sell.

It's wonderful to note that this is the same game with some extra gimmicks. Nothing is developed in this game, nothing feels new or refreshed, nothing introduced as a new concept. it has good feel and great graphics, but if the gameplay is redundant, the music is redundant, the game feels old, what's the damn point of even buying it. I don't recommend if you don't want more of the same from cash-cow mario team.
i love this game on the wii u i havent 100%ed it and its also my first i want new super lugei u but this will do as one of my favorite wii u games

This is a horrible game. It is just the older ones with different stages. At least with the wii version the final boss was unique in how to defeat him. But in this game he is just a little bit bigger. The stages aren't good, The multiplayer focus isn't great.
only better than new mario on wii .. where are all the good ideas and improvement from old games ? i want a nice painted game like supermario world  or mario land 2
This game is basically, just New Super Mario Bros Wii, but on the Wii U, it's just too repetitive

I am very disappointed in Nintendo for making this game.  It plays almost exactly the same as the New SMB for Wii--same sound effects, same music, same graphics (except the upgrade to HD which is not that dramatic given the graphical style of mario games.  Miyamoto said he wanted to release a mario game with each new console, which I agree with, but please do so by making a completely new game, not by just increasing the difficultu and adding a few things.  I don't need my mario games to become ever more complex.  I just need them to be FUN.  And New SMB Wii U is simply not fun if you've already played New SMB Wii.

This game is fun, but I'm giving this a low score for two reasons:

1) This game is basically the same as the original Wii game, just with different levels and a few slightly new features.

2) The whole reason that Luigi exists is because Nintendo needed a second character for 2-player NES games. They also created Waluigi out of thin-air solely because Wario needed a doubles partner in Mario Tennis. So why on earth are we stuck playing with TWO Toads? Why hasn't Nintendo used this opportunity to introduce two new characters to join Mario and Luigi as the "stars" of the game?

Instead of giving us two brand new characters that could be spun off in a ton of other games (not to mention could help increase the character stable for sporting games; who else is sick of all these "baby" characters in Mario Kart? amiright?) they completely blow it and give us not one, but TWO, Toad characters. LAME

Oh well, if you've never played the original Wii game, then this would probably be a fun pickup. If you have played the original Wii game, there really is no reason to get this. It probably should have just been cheap DLC, since, again, it's basically the exact same game, just different levels.

I just recently bought a Wii U with New Super Mario Bros U.  This has been the most poorly constructed Mario game that I can remember.  I enjoyed the New Super Mario Bros for the Wii.  This new Wii U version is the EXACT same thing.  The graphics look identical and the game play feels identical.  If anything, this game is less creative than the former version.  New Super Mario Bros U is a fast cash grabbing solution to a struggling console.

nintendo-switch-2-welcome-tour_with_meta.review:
Gameplay Mechanics - 9/10: Showcases possibilities in many curious ways, but wish there were more to explore.
Graphics & Visuals - 9/10: Depicts real world items pretty well.
Narrative - 2/10: I mean we could get a lot more type of inner games to explore possibilities, not those lame demos.
Sound & Music - 9/10: Simple and suits well.
Replayability - 3/10: Could be explored only once, you could try to beat your own records in lame games.
Innovation - 7/10: Actually the idea behind all of this is quite cool, the realization is poor in the end.
Technical Performance - 10/10: Perfect.
Art Direction/Design - 9/10: Cute and smooth art style, very appealing and seamless.
Monetization/Value - 0/10: That's a dumb decision to sell it...

Total - 6/10

This is a disgrace to Nintendo. It forces you to collect all the stamps before you can move on to the next scene, you can't freely access the instruction manual, there are outdated and idiotic quizzes, uninteresting mini-games, and ridiculous scene displays. As a Nintendo fan, I felt humiliated after playing it. As many have said, it should have been free, but Nintendo's arrogance in this generation prevented them from doing so, instead making users pay to experience a pile of crap. Making such a product makes me furious. I don't recommend buying it; it will only waste your precious time.

It's decent enough to learn more about the console, but it should be free. The demos and minigames could be better, the latter ones overuse mouse mode. Plus, it forces you to buy extra accessories for some of these experiences. The biggest problem with this "game" is that Astro's Playroom already exists and came free with the console.
I actually enjoyed learning about how the switch was made. Some of the mini games are pretty fun too
Are you serious ? Charging ANYTHING for this is absolutely baffling. Nintendo is the worst company in the world
A complete Joke to charge money for this. Bland, uninspired, boring and a reminder that Nintendo became a soulless Company that doesn't even try to hide the fact.
this game is like scrambled eggs it looks like gosh darn piss and it is gosh darn piss
this is a borring/basic tutorial and wtf 10 dollars!?!?!?!??!?!!?!?!?!?!?!?
ITS way to hard for Kids. My Kid cried because this Game was way to hard for him and even i didn't make it

I haven’t finished the game because the game literally doesn’t let me. 

This game is supposed to show the user everything about the Switch 2, its gimmicks, specialties and more. A tech demo you have to pay 10 euros for. I honestly didn’t really mind spending a few bucks on a game that, in my opinion, should be free, but now I fully regret it. You can play the game for quite a while until you get to specific mini-games that require your monitor or TV to meet certain requirements, or that you need a specific accessory for. This is outrageous!So now you’ve paid for a game you can’t even finish because you need extra requirements on top of it! NOWHERE on the Nintendo eShop does it say that this is necessary (I'm talking about Monitor/TV requierments, it does say you might need certain accessories). So what looks like a 10-euro game stacks up to a 500+ euro game if you don’t already have all the accessories and equipment requirements! Absolutely outrageous! This game should have come free with the Switch 2!

Who decided this undercooked instruction manual is worth $10 + all the console accessories, if you want the whole experience of this literal tech demo. Wii Sports, Nintendo Land, and Bomberman Act Zero on the Xbox 360. outshine this game in every single category.
10 dollars for a tech demo, 10 dollars for a tech demo when deltarunen chapters / and 2 are free

They're charging money for the instructions manual of a console that already had a big price bump.
And it's not even a good experience, it's just tedium, tests and a couple of 'barely' minigames. What a joke of a product.
Should have been free. Even then, it still wouldn't be worth it since you can't 100% the game without purchasing other accessories that hasn't even come out yet.
As a pack in game I would have added a point or two as it is it’s a serviceable manual without the usual Nintendo charm.

€10 for a Wiki page, no thanks
And you need ALL THE ACCESSORIES (even the joy con wheel) to complete the game...

it is a nice game for those who are interessted in technical specifications and want to learn more about there console, all in all a good game, i had my fun with this game, should be free nethertheless
Definitely one of the more lamer games Nintendo have made...beat it in less than 2 hours. Two thumbs down.
If theres any game that does not deserve the hate it gets its this gem this is a relaxing experience with super fun activities.

An incisive academic journey that’s absolutely worth the price of admission. If you think it should’ve been free, you clearly don’t grasp what it’s for. Want a free manual? Check the box — it won’t tell you why anything was done the way it was, and it definitely won’t walk you through it interactively. "But hey, it’s free"
The switch 2 welcome tour is a meh tech demo That makes it even more meh because of its price. Astro bot is free and is better than this worthless scam.

Man I really wanted to like this. Outside of the very very very obvious flaw that it should have been included it’s just kinda mostly meh. 

The good: design ideas are fun of exploring the device, nice clean design. It’s kinda fun finding all the components.

Bad: mini games are ridiculously uninspired, characters around are super generic, you find random generic items (why not find like a game boy or something fun instead of like a shoe). The quizzes are just kinda time consuming,

The entire thing feels like some beta idea that should have been fleshed outs. How are 90% of these demos not like an alpha build thing, they’re devoid of any character or care. Someone slapped them together in an afternoon it looks **** not do them like Mario party style??

Man what a disappointment
Charge money for this game is a straight disrespect to all of Nintendo's fans and customers

Charging me for a welcome tour is not very welcoming. I think this is the last the true showcase of how far Nintendo's greed has gone, and how it will never end, so long as we support them.

They clearly no longer care about user experience and just want to exploit us for every penny they can get.

An infinite sea of trash on a trash platform made of trashy trash from my trash bin.  Seriously, this is insulting to have to pay for, and Nintendo needs to get its head out of its butt if it thinks this is worth paying for.
The game itself is an 8/10, it's fun, it's quiet difficult to 100%, it has a lot of content, but the price, even if it's low, it could be cool to play for free when purchasing a Switch 2

I am mad at myself for buying this. Nintendo charged so much more than anything previously for its new console, and then decided that for their promotional tech demo game to show off the features and functions of it, they would charge another $10. It's got to be one of the worst, most greedy looks for a game company in a long time. 

That said, I am absolutely the type of person who loves to really take the time to explore and learn about all of the unique and cool new aspects of a piece of tech that I bought, and Welcome Tour really gives a thorough walkthrough of everything about the system in a very palatable way. I enjoyed learning about some of the thoughtful physical design aspects of the controllers and intentional placements of inner components of the system's components just as much as I liked the tech demos that showed off the new or improved capabilities of the system. It gives you a chance to experience what the system has to offer visually, audibly, or physically, through cool little demonstrations. 

The actual minigames, which are likely the only part that would merit charging money for this game, might have been my least favorite part, as most of them require you to constantly shuffle between control styles, docked/handheld mode, or to use various accessories. You cannot actually complete all of the challenges without owning the appropriate accessories in some cases, either - despite them even providing a way to "skip" those challenges... but this does not actually give you progress towards completion.

In all, I did enjoy strolling around the game system's surfaces, learning some cool little insights into the development of the system, and I did leave with an added respect and appreciation for how many decisions and careful considerations had to be weighed in creating this next-gen hybrid console. However, the frustrations with minigame progression and requirements to own extra accessories to finish it are small marks against the game as a whole. The horrifically greedy and tasteless decision to charge $10 for this tech demo is what brings the marks down to a 5 here. 

Final recommendation: If you absolutely love learning about your new tech, and want to do so in a creative and somewhat entertaining way, go for it. Otherwise, please do not support this type of corporate behavior and skip this.

Disappointingly Underwhelming – Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour Misses the MarkI had high hopes going into the Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour, especially with all the hype around the next generation of hybrid gaming. Unfortunately, the event felt more like a missed opportunity than a true celebration of innovation.First off, the demo stations were painfully limited. For a console that’s supposedly launching with a powerful new chipset and an OLED Pro display, you’d think Nintendo would let fans spend more time with actual gameplay. Instead, we were stuck watching curated trailers or playing brief, scripted demos that didn’t showcase much more than what’s already available online. Where was the promised leap in performance?Second, the titles available were either ports or sequels that looked nearly indistinguishable from what we’ve already played on the original Switch. Sure, Mario Kart X and Zelda: Echoes of the Past looked polished, but they didn't scream “next-gen” the way you'd expect. And shockingly, there was no mention of backward compatibility—a baffling omission that left a lot of returning fans confused and **** staff, while enthusiastic, didn’t seem prepared to answer deeper questions about the hardware, cloud features, or digital game transfer. The few tech details we got felt carefully managed, almost as if Nintendo was holding back instead of building excitement.Lastly, the merchandise and fan engagement felt shallow—lots of QR codes and photo ops, but little in the way of meaningful interaction. For an event that’s meant to welcome both new and longtime fans, it felt more like a glorified commercial than an immersive experience.Nintendo has the magic, but this tour didn’t capture it. Here’s hoping the actual console release will do what this event couldn’t: convince us the Switch 2 is more than just an incremental upgrade.
Great game to explore Nintendo Switch 2! I like to see how is going on switch 2. It is kinds fun when you play with open mind.

DO NOT BUY THIS. I bought this and while I don't believe I was "robbed" this is really not worth anything above like 1 dollar, it is full of shallow tech-demos, the ONLY reason you would find any value in this game is if you are really interested in technical stuff, and even then it doesn't consistantly deliver even that.
To get more into my experience, there were some tech demos I liked, more specifically the upscaling tech demo (which I found was the best tech demo I have seen for upscalers), some facts I liked about how the console was engineered because I'm into engineering myself, but I must be clear, THIS IS NOT A GAME, if you are looking for a game just look elsewhere, if you are starved for a 120 fps experience on switch 2 this doesn't deliver anything at all, and pretty much ANYTHING else please just watch a video on youtube.

Genuinely had a lot of fun with this. Enjoyed learning about the console and the mini games were fun. I'm definitely in the minority and was sceptical before purchasing, but I really had fun with this game.
This game is only a small introduction with tutorials or text. It's like here is switch 2 that can do this things and most is text only some small Minigames.

While this “Game” (Tech demo) does present some interesting insights into how certain parts of the controller were designed (I.E using magnets and using the Sr and Sl buttons as steel to attach to the side of the console this should be FREE information on a FREE tech demo.£8 (Or any price for that matter) is a absolute travesty and turns what could of been a fun tech demo into a overpriced piece of garbage.

For comparison, for that same price, I could buy Undertale on steam for that price which is a full game with multiple endings, interesting character and deep lore. Game is “affordable” because AAA companies have gotten gamers used to paying £60 standard for a new game but take a look at indies and you shall see the value is inflated and experiences like this aren’t worth your time.
The biggest childlike overreaction in gaming I've seen yet. Far better put together and more enjoyable than you're being told.

This is quite a nothing burger. Charging any amount of money for this shows yet again, Nintendo's refusal to understand where their product actually sits in quality. The mini-games were mildly interesting but I have no interest in going back to any of them and since I don't have every accessory, I can't even finish some of the mini games. If this was packed in for free with the console, I'd say this is interesting, albeit lazy. Since it's a product with its own price tag, I have to say it's an embarrassment.

Welcome Tour isn’t really a game, it’s really the equivalent of bonus features on a Blu-ray disguised as a game. Some people love that, some don’t : and that’s precisely why I would’ve made a terrible pack-in title ! 

This is bonus content for people who want bonus content. If it was a pack-in people would’ve complained about it anyway and asked Nintendo to remove it from the console to lower the price.

However, if you like behind-the-scenes type of content and/or loved science museums when you were a kid... this is pure gold. I was genuinely fascinated by all the "design secrets" Nintendo was willing to share. I would often pick up my console or dock to marvel at a tiny detail made to make our life easier after seeing it in one of the information panel.


The game is beautiful and fun, the dialogues are charming and I really didn’t mind the "stamp collecting" thing. I wasn’t blown away by the technical demos and mini-games, but they were fun (some were terribly addictive and challenging, though !). It shows what could be possible in the future and I hope devs will actually use the console’s features more than Switch 1’s game.

All in all, the drama surrounding it was incredibly stupid because that’s truly a beautifully crafted experience that’s well worth its price. Get it if you find the concept appealing. Ignore it, if you think it’s not for you.

Disappointingly Underwhelming – Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour Misses the MarkI had high hopes going into the Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour, especially with all the hype around the next generation of hybrid gaming. Unfortunately, the event felt more like a missed opportunity than a true celebration of innovation.First off, the demo stations were painfully limited. For a console that’s supposedly launching with a powerful new chipset and an OLED Pro display, you’d think Nintendo would let fans spend more time with actual gameplay. Instead, we were stuck watching curated trailers or playing brief, scripted demos that didn’t showcase much more than what’s already available online. Where was the promised leap in performance?Second, the titles available were either ports or sequels that looked nearly indistinguishable from what we’ve already played on the original Switch. Sure, Mario Kart X and Zelda: Echoes of the Past looked polished, but they didn't scream “next-gen” the way you'd expect. And shockingly, there was no mention of backward compatibility—a baffling omission that left a lot of returning fans confused and **** staff, while enthusiastic, didn’t seem prepared to answer deeper questions about the hardware, cloud features, or digital game transfer. The few tech details we got felt carefully managed, almost as if Nintendo was holding back instead of building excitement.Lastly, the merchandise and fan engagement felt shallow—lots of QR codes and photo ops, but little in the way of meaningful interaction. For an event that’s meant to welcome both new and longtime fans, it felt more like a glorified commercial than an immersive experience.Nintendo has the magic, but this tour didn’t capture it. Here’s hoping the actual console release will do what this event couldn’t: convince us the Switch 2 is more than just an incremental upgrade.
Great game to explore Nintendo Switch 2! I like to see how is going on switch 2. It is kinds fun when you play with open mind.

DO NOT BUY THIS. I bought this and while I don't believe I was "robbed" this is really not worth anything above like 1 dollar, it is full of shallow tech-demos, the ONLY reason you would find any value in this game is if you are really interested in technical stuff, and even then it doesn't consistantly deliver even that.
To get more into my experience, there were some tech demos I liked, more specifically the upscaling tech demo (which I found was the best tech demo I have seen for upscalers), some facts I liked about how the console was engineered because I'm into engineering myself, but I must be clear, THIS IS NOT A GAME, if you are looking for a game just look elsewhere, if you are starved for a 120 fps experience on switch 2 this doesn't deliver anything at all, and pretty much ANYTHING else please just watch a video on youtube.

Genuinely had a lot of fun with this. Enjoyed learning about the console and the mini games were fun. I'm definitely in the minority and was sceptical before purchasing, but I really had fun with this game.
This game is only a small introduction with tutorials or text. It's like here is switch 2 that can do this things and most is text only some small Minigames.

While this “Game” (Tech demo) does present some interesting insights into how certain parts of the controller were designed (I.E using magnets and using the Sr and Sl buttons as steel to attach to the side of the console this should be FREE information on a FREE tech demo.£8 (Or any price for that matter) is a absolute travesty and turns what could of been a fun tech demo into a overpriced piece of garbage.

For comparison, for that same price, I could buy Undertale on steam for that price which is a full game with multiple endings, interesting character and deep lore. Game is “affordable” because AAA companies have gotten gamers used to paying £60 standard for a new game but take a look at indies and you shall see the value is inflated and experiences like this aren’t worth your time.
The biggest childlike overreaction in gaming I've seen yet. Far better put together and more enjoyable than you're being told.

This is quite a nothing burger. Charging any amount of money for this shows yet again, Nintendo's refusal to understand where their product actually sits in quality. The mini-games were mildly interesting but I have no interest in going back to any of them and since I don't have every accessory, I can't even finish some of the mini games. If this was packed in for free with the console, I'd say this is interesting, albeit lazy. Since it's a product with its own price tag, I have to say it's an embarrassment.

Welcome Tour isn’t really a game, it’s really the equivalent of bonus features on a Blu-ray disguised as a game. Some people love that, some don’t : and that’s precisely why I would’ve made a terrible pack-in title ! 

This is bonus content for people who want bonus content. If it was a pack-in people would’ve complained about it anyway and asked Nintendo to remove it from the console to lower the price.

However, if you like behind-the-scenes type of content and/or loved science museums when you were a kid... this is pure gold. I was genuinely fascinated by all the "design secrets" Nintendo was willing to share. I would often pick up my console or dock to marvel at a tiny detail made to make our life easier after seeing it in one of the information panel.


The game is beautiful and fun, the dialogues are charming and I really didn’t mind the "stamp collecting" thing. I wasn’t blown away by the technical demos and mini-games, but they were fun (some were terribly addictive and challenging, though !). It shows what could be possible in the future and I hope devs will actually use the console’s features more than Switch 1’s game.

All in all, the drama surrounding it was incredibly stupid because that’s truly a beautifully crafted experience that’s well worth its price. Get it if you find the concept appealing. Ignore it, if you think it’s not for you.

Gameplay Mechanics - 9/10: Showcases possibilities in many curious ways, but wish there were more to explore.
Graphics & Visuals - 9/10: Depicts real world items pretty well.
Narrative - 2/10: I mean we could get a lot more type of inner games to explore possibilities, not those lame demos.
Sound & Music - 9/10: Simple and suits well.
Replayability - 3/10: Could be explored only once, you could try to beat your own records in lame games.
Innovation - 7/10: Actually the idea behind all of this is quite cool, the realization is poor in the end.
Technical Performance - 10/10: Perfect.
Art Direction/Design - 9/10: Cute and smooth art style, very appealing and seamless.
Monetization/Value - 0/10: That's a dumb decision to sell it...

Total - 6/10

I am mad at myself for buying this. Nintendo charged so much more than anything previously for its new console, and then decided that for their promotional tech demo game to show off the features and functions of it, they would charge another $10. It's got to be one of the worst, most greedy looks for a game company in a long time. 

That said, I am absolutely the type of person who loves to really take the time to explore and learn about all of the unique and cool new aspects of a piece of tech that I bought, and Welcome Tour really gives a thorough walkthrough of everything about the system in a very palatable way. I enjoyed learning about some of the thoughtful physical design aspects of the controllers and intentional placements of inner components of the system's components just as much as I liked the tech demos that showed off the new or improved capabilities of the system. It gives you a chance to experience what the system has to offer visually, audibly, or physically, through cool little demonstrations. 

The actual minigames, which are likely the only part that would merit charging money for this game, might have been my least favorite part, as most of them require you to constantly shuffle between control styles, docked/handheld mode, or to use various accessories. You cannot actually complete all of the challenges without owning the appropriate accessories in some cases, either - despite them even providing a way to "skip" those challenges... but this does not actually give you progress towards completion.

In all, I did enjoy strolling around the game system's surfaces, learning some cool little insights into the development of the system, and I did leave with an added respect and appreciation for how many decisions and careful considerations had to be weighed in creating this next-gen hybrid console. However, the frustrations with minigame progression and requirements to own extra accessories to finish it are small marks against the game as a whole. The horrifically greedy and tasteless decision to charge $10 for this tech demo is what brings the marks down to a 5 here. 

Final recommendation: If you absolutely love learning about your new tech, and want to do so in a creative and somewhat entertaining way, go for it. Otherwise, please do not support this type of corporate behavior and skip this.

As an engineer, I thoroughly enjoyed this tour of the Nintendo Switch 2. It allowed me to see the new gimmicks of this hardware and the improvements over the Switch 1. The demo was fine and impressive, but it lacked an online scoreboard and some replayability. Overall, this tour served its purpose.

$10? For this masterpiece? That’s not a price — that’s charity. ‘Welcome Tour’ isn’t a game, it’s an experience. It taught me more about my Switch 2 than four years of tech YouTubers ever could. The rumble feedback? Transcendental. The design breakdowns? Oscar-worthy. I walked in expecting a tutorial — I left spiritually fulfilled. Nintendo didn’t have to go this hard… but they did. GOTY 2025, and somehow the best bargain of the decade. If you skip this, you’re skipping the soul of the system

GAME OF THE YEAR! - Just kidding. But this is a relaxing, fun, and interesting piece of software for the Nintendo Switch 2.

The game itself is structured like a real-life museum of the Switch 2, which is unique and cute. The stamp rally, while quite obvious sometimes, gets interesting the longer you play. This is also the same case with the quizzes and insights. There are technical details that go very deep and are quite rare to see in a Nintendo game. This extends to the minigames, which show off mouse controls, GL/GR buttons, gyro controls, and my favorite - a game of Twister where you need to put 10 fingers on the touchscreen at once. The tech demos are quite interesting too, showing DLSS/Super Resolution, HD Rumble 2 -which is so much better than the original, and more. One of my favorites was having to find the singular pixel on the Switch 2's 1080p screen. It has a nice presentation and is worth the 10 bucks. Obviously it should have been a free pack-in title, but for those interested in Nintendo's quirkier romps, this is sure to be a good time.
I don’t understand why this game gets so much bad reviews. It’s 10$ should be free I know. But for what it is a guide it is pretty good. It’s fun to play and the minigames are great.

Its job is to tell people about how the switch 2 works and what it can do, and it does a good job at it. The mini games are fun, and the tech demos let you know how good the Switch 2 is. And yes, it was interesting to find out how the switch 2 does everything. It does a good job as a tech demo. But as an actual game it could do a better job. You get all the stamps and all the game says is nice job. There's no credits, no post game content, no nothing. You get nothing for beating or even 100% beating this game. This game is clearly for a specific group of people and for them it's a great game. I am not in that group of people. I've been defending this game since day one and wanted to give it a good score but that lack of an ending makes it hard. It's still a good game with a fair price and for a tech demo it's pretty solid. But as a game it's a little lacking.

It’s no Astro’s Playroom, but if you want an actual demonstration of all the parts of the Switch, it’s actually better in that respect than Astro’s Playroom was for the PS5. (And really what’s the point of making a free game to show off all the features of the new gamepad, when most of the actual PS5 games barely make any use of them?)
